ANatett satisfactory account of many parts of Celestial Mechamcs lather than an exhaustive trcatment of any special part. The aim has bcen to prescnt the work so as to attain logical sequcnce, to makeit progressivcly more difficult, and to give tho various subjccts the relative prominence which thoir scientific and cducational importance deserves. In short, the aim has bccn to piepaie such a book that one who has had the necessary mathematical tiainmg may obtain from il in a ielalively short time and by the easiest stcps a sufficiently bioad and jusl viow of the whole subject to cnable him to stop with much of real value im his posscsson, or to pursue to the best advantage any particulai porlion he may choosc.

In carrying out the plan of this work it has been necessary to give an intrpduction to the Problem of Threo BodicsThis is not only one of tho justly celcbrated problems of Celestial Mechanics, but it has bccome of special intorest in rccent timcs through the researchcs of Iill, Poinoaié,and Darwin. The thcory of absolute perturbations is the central subjcct in mathematical Astronomy,and such a work as this would be inexousably deficieni if il did not give this theory a promineni place. A chapter has beon devoted to gcometrical considerations on perturbalions Although these methods are of almost no use im coimputing, yet they fuinish in & simple mannor a clear insight into tho nature of the problem, and are of the highest valuo to beginnors. The fundamental principles of tho analytical methods have been given with gonsiderable completeness, but many of tho details in dovoloping thc formulas have bcen omilted in order that the sizo of tho book might not defeat the objoct for which it has been prepared. The theory of orbits has not beengiven the unduly prominent position which it has oooupicd in thiscountry，doubtloss due to the infuence of Watson's oxcellent treatiso on this subjeot

The method of treatment has bcen to state all problems in advance and, where tho transformations aro long, to give an outline of tho stops whioh are to be made. The oxprossion “ordor of small quantities” has not been uscd oxcopt when applied to power serieg in oxplicit parameterg, thus giving tho work all the definiteness and simplicity which are characteristic of operations with powei senes. This is exemplfied particularly in tho chapter on perturbationsCare has been taken to make note at all places where assumptions have been introduced or umjustified methods employed,for it is only by seeing whero the points of possible weakness are that impiovcments can be made‘The frequent references throughout the text and the biblographies at the ends of the chapteis, though by no means oxhaustive,are suffcient to direct one m fuither icading to important sources of mformation

This volume is the outgiowth of $\clubsuit$ course of lectures given annually by the author at the University of Chicago during the last six years. These lectures have been open to senior college students and to graduate students who havo not had the equivalent of this work.They have been taken by students of Astronomy， by many makmg Mathematics their major work, and by some who, though specializing in quite distinct lincs,have desiied to get an idea of the processes by means of which astronomcrs interpret and predict celestial phenomena, Thus thoy have served to give many an idea of the methods of investigation and the results attained in Celestial Mechanics, and have prepared some for a detailed study extending into the various branchos of modern investigationsThe object of the work, the subjects covered,and the methods of treatment seem to have been amply justified by this experience.

Mr.A C Lunn,MA.,has read tho entiro manuseript with great care and a thorough insight into the subjects treatcd. His numerous corrections and suggestions havo added groatly to the accuracy and the method of treatmcnt in many places. Professor Ormond Stone has read the proofs of the first four chapters and the sixth. His experience as an investigator and as a teacher has made his criticisms and suggestions invaluable. Mr W. O.Beal, M.A., has read the proofs of the whole book with great attention and he is responsible for many improvements. The author degires to express his sincerest thanks to all these gontlomen for the willingness and the effectiveness with which they have devoted so much of their time to this work.

# PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION.

T which has been followcd is the same as that of the firsl cdition, becausc it was found that it satisfies a real need nol only in this country,for whoso students il was primarily written, but also in Euiope. In spito of all lemptations its clementary charactor has been preservcd, and it has not bcen greatly cnlarged. Very many improvemonts have been made, partly on tho suggestion of numcious astronomers and mathomaticians,and it is hoped that it will be found more worthy of the favor with which il has so far been reccived.

The most important single change is in the discussion of the methods of detormining orbits.This subjeet logically follows the Problem of Two Bodies,and it is much moio elcmentary in character than the Problem of Threo Bodies and the Theory of PorturbationsFor thcso reasons it was placcd in chaptor VI. The subject matter has also been vory much changed． Tho mothods of Laplaco and Gauss, on which all other mothods of general applicabihty arc moro or loss directly based, aro both given.Tho standard modos of prosentation have noi been followed becauso, however well thoy may be adapted to practico, thoy aro not noted for mathematical clarity. Bcsides,thero is no Inck of cxcollont works giving dotails in thc original forms and modolg of computation. The other changes and additions of importanoo are in the chaptérs on the Problom of Two Bodics, tho Problom of Threo Bodics,and in that on Geometiical Consideration of Porturbations.

It is a pleasure to make spccial aoknowledgmont of asistance to my colleague Profossor W. D. MacMillan and to Mr. L. A. Hopkins who have rend thoe ontire proofs not only once bul sovoral times,and who have mado important suggestions and havo pointed out many dofects that othorwiso would have escnped notico. They aro largcly respongiblo for whatover oxoellence of form the book may possoss.

# INTRODUCTION TO CELESTIAL MECHANICS

# CHAPTER I.

# FUNDAMENTAL PRINCIPLES AND DEFINITIONS

1. Elements and Laws. The problems of cvery science are expressible in certain terms which will be designatcd as elements, and depend upon certan prnceples and laws for thcir solution. Tho elements arise fiom thc vory natuie of thc subject considered, and are expressed or implied in the formulalion of the problems tteated. The principles and laws are the relations which are known or are assumed to exist among the various elcmcnts. They aro inductions from cxperiments,or deductions from previously accepted principles and laws, or simply agroements.

An explcit statement in the beginning of the typo of problems which will be treated,and an cnumcration of tho elements which they involve,and of tho principles and laws which relate to them, will lead to clearncss of exposition. In order to obtain  completo understanding of the character of the conclusions whioh are icached,it would be necessary to makea philosophical discussion of thc reality of the elements,and of the origin and characler of the principles and laws. These qucstions cannol be enleied into hcre because of the difficulty and comploxity of metaphysical speculataons.It is not to be undorstood that such investigations alo not of value；they forever lead back to simpler and more undeniable assumptions upon whioh to base al reasoning.

The method of procedure in this work will ncccssarily be to accept as truo cortain fundamental elements and laws without cntering in detail inlo the questions of their reality or validity. It will bc suffciont to consider whethcr they are definitions or have been inferred from experienco，and to point out that thoy havo beon abundantly verified in their applications.They will be accopted with confidence,and their consequoncos will be derived, in the subjects treated, so far as the scopo and limitg of tho work will allow.

2.Problems Treated. The motions of $\mathfrak { a }$ material particle subject to & central force of any soit whatever will be bricfly consdered It will be shown from the conclusions reached m this discussion,and from the obseryed motions of the planots and their satcllites,that Newton's law of gravitation holds true in thc solar system. The character of the motion of the binary slars shows that tho probabilties are very great that it operatcs m thcm also, and thai it may well be termed “thc law of universal gravitation"Ths conclusion is confirmcd by the spcctioscope, which proves that the familiar chemical elements of our solar syslom exist in tho stars also

In particular,the motions of two frcc homogeneous sphores subject only to their mutual attractions and stailing from arbitrary imtial conditions will be mvcstigated, and thon thcir molions will be discussed when they aic subject to disturbing influences of various sorts. The cssential features of perturbationB arising from tho action of $\mathbf { a }$ third body will be developed,both from a gcometrical and an analytical point of view. There aie two somcwhat dffcrent cases. One is lhat in which thc motion of a satellitc around $\mathfrak { a }$ planet is pertubcd by the sun；and the second is that in which the motion of one planct around the sun is porturbed by another planct.

Another class of pioblems which arises is tho doterminalion of tho orbits of unknown bodies from the observations of thcir diroctions at different epochs, mado from a body whoso motion is known. That is, the theories of the orbita of comels and plan" etoids will be based upon observations of thcir appareni positions made from the earth. This incompleto outlme of the quostions to be trcated is sufficient for tho enumeralion of the cloments employed.

3.Enumeration of the Principal Elements.‘In the discussion of the problems considcred in this work it will be necessary to employ tho following clements:

$\mathbf { \rho } ( a )$ Real numbers,and complex numbers incidenlally in the golution of certain problems. (b) Space of thrce dimensions, possessing tho same properticg in every direction. (c) Trme of one dimension, which will be taken as tho independent variable. (d) Mass,having the ordinary properties of inertia, etc., which are postulated in elementary Physics,

(e) Fore, with the content that thc same toim has in Physics.

Positive numbcrs arise in Arithmetie,and positive, negative, and complex numbers,in AlgebraSpace appears first as an csscntial eloment m Geometry.Timo appcais first as an cssential clement in Kncmatics. Mass and forco appcar first and must be consideied as cssential elementg in physical pioblems. No defintlons of these familiai clements aio necessary herc.

4. Enumeration of the Principles and Laws. In representing the valious physical magnitudes by numbeis, ccitatn agrcements must be made as to what shall bo consrdercd positive,and what negativc.Thc axioms of orclinaiy Geometry will bc considered as being tiuc.

Tho fundamental principles upon which all work in Theoretical Mechanics may be made to depend are Newlon's three Axroms, or Laws of MotronThe fnst two laws wore known by Galleo and Iuyghens,although thoy wele for the first timo announced together in all their completencss by Nowton in the Prncpia, in 1686. These laws are as follows.\*

LAW I.Every body contnues in zts slate of rest, or of uniform moton ina straghi line,unlessit zscompelled to change that state by a force impressed upon rt.

LAw II The rale of change of motion zs proportional to the force zmpressed,and takes place in the drrection of the straight hne in which the force acts

LAW III. To every aclron there is an equal and opponte reactron; or,the mutual actions of two bodies are always equal and opposrtely drected.

5. Nature of the Laws of Motion. Newton calls the Laws of Motion Axioms,and aftor giving cach,mukcs a fow romarks conceining its import. Later writors, among whom aro Thomson and Tait,t regard them as inferonces from exporienco, but accept Newton's formulation of them as practically final,and adopt them in tho precise form in which they wcre given in the Prncipa. A number of Continental writers,among whom is Dr.Ernest Mach, havo given profound thought to the fundatmental prineiples of

\*Other fundamental Iawe may be,and indoed havo been,employod,but they involve more dificult mathemationl piinciples nt tho very BtartThoy aro uch na d'Alembort's prineiplo, Iamilton's prinoiple, and tho ystema of Kirohhoff,ach, rtz,Boltzmto

Mechanics,and have concluded that they ae not only nduchons or simplyconvenos,butthat Newton'sstatementofthemissome what redundant, and lacks scientific dectness_and simpleity. There is no suggestion,howover, that Newton's Laws of otion are not in harmony with odinary astionomioal expeiience,or that they cannot be made the basis for Celestial MechanicsBut in some branches of Physics,particulaily n Elcctricity and Lght, certain phenomena aie not fully consistent with tho Newtonian principles,and they have recently ledEinsteinand othes to the development of the so-called Prnceple of Relatvty The astronomical consequences of this modification of thc prineiples of Mechanics are vey slght unless the time under consideration is very long,and,whether they ale true or not,they cannot be considered in an introduction to the subject.

6.Remarks on the First Law of Motion. In the fist law the statement that a body subject to no foiccs moves with unfoim motion,may be regarded asa definiton of tme.For,otherwiso, it is impled that theie exists some mcthod of mcasuring timo im which motion is not involvedNow it is $\mathfrak { a }$ fact that in all the devices actually used for measuning time this pail of the law is a fundamental assumption. For example,it is assumed that tho earth rotates at $\mathfrak { a }$ uniform iate bccause therc is no forcc acting upon it which changes the rotation sensibly.\*

The second part of the law,which affirms that the motion is in a strazght lne when the body is subject to no forccs,may bo takon as defining a straight line,if it 1s assumed that it is possble to determine when ${ \mathfrak { a } } ,$ bodyis subject to no forces;or,it may be taken as showing, together with the first parl,whclher or not forces are acting,if it is assumed that it is possible to give an independent definition of a straight line Either altornativo leads to toublesome diffculties when an attempt is made to employ strict and consistent defimtions.

7.Remarks on the Second Law of Motion. In the sccond law the statement that the rate of change of motion is pioportional to the force impressed,may be regarded as a definition of the relation between foree and matter by means of which the magnitude of $\mathfrak { a }$ force,or th'e amount of matter in a body can be measuied,aocording as one or the other 1s supposed to be independently known. By rate of change of motion is mcant ihe rate of change of velocity multiplied by the mass of tho body movedTlus is usualy called the rate of change of momentum,and thc ideas of the second law may be expicsscd by saying,the sate of change of momentum is proportronal to the foice rmmessed and lakes place in the drecton of the stazght lne in whch the force actsOi,the acceleranon of motion of a body rs derectly moportonal to the force to which et rs subject,and inver sely propoitronal to rts mass,and tahes place in the drection 'n which the foice acts

It may appcar at fist thought that foice can be measured without rcfeience to velooity genorated,and it is true in a sonse. For cxample,thc force with which gravity dawsa body downwaid 1s frequently measuicd by tho stretching of a coiled spring,or the intensity of magnetic action can bo measuied by the loision of a fiberBut it will be noticed m all cases of this kind thal the law of reaction of thc machino has been dcteimned in some other wayThis may not have been dncctly by velocitics geneiated, but it ulumately leads back to il.Il is woithy of nole m this conncction that all the units of absolute foicc,as the dyne,contain explicitly in their dofinitions thc idca of velocity generated.

In lho statemcnt of tho sccond law it is impled that the ffect of $\mathfrak { a }$ force is cxactly ‘ho samc in whatever condition of resl or of motion thc body may bo,and to whalovei olhci foices it may be subject. The changc of molion of a body acled upon by a number of forccs is the same al tho end of an intei val of timo as if cach foico acted scparalcly for thc same timeIonco the implication in tho sccond law is,sf any number of forces acl simultaneously on a body,whether it is at rest or in motron,each force produces the same total change of momentum thal it would produce yf it alone acted on the body al rest.It is apparent that this prnciple leads to great simplifications of mochanical problems,for in accordanco with it tho effccts of the vaiious forces can be considered sepuiately.

Newton derived tho paallelogiam of forces fom tho second law of motion.\*IIe roasoncd that as forccs auc measured by the accclcrations which they producc,the resultant of,say,two forces should bc measuied by tho resultant of thoir accelerationsSinca an acceleration has magnitudo.and direction it can be represonted by ${ \mathfrak { a } }$ directed line,or vector.Tho resultant of two forccs wil then bo repiesented by the diagonal of a paralllogram, of which two adjacent sidcs ropresent tho two forces.

\* Princepia,Cor.1.to the Laws of Motion

One of the most frequent applications of thc paiallelogram of forces is in the subject of Statics,which,in itself,does not involve thc ideas of motion and time In it the idea of mass can also be entirely eliminated. Newton's proof of the parallclogram of forces has been objected to on the ground thal it rcquires thc introduction of the fundamental conceptions of a much moie complcated science than the one in which il Is employed. Among the demonstrations which avoid this objectionable feature is one due to Poisson,\* which has foi its fundamental assumption the axiom that the resultant of two equal forccs applicd at & point is in the line of the bisector of the anglo which they make with each other. Then the magnitude of the resultant is dcrived,and by simple processes the general law is obtauned.

8.Remarks on the Third Law of Motion. Thc fist two of Newton's laws are sufficient for tho determination of the motion of one body subjeot to any number of known foices;but another prinoiple is needed when the investigation conceins the motion of a system of two or more bodies subject to their mutual interactions. The third law qf motion expresses precisely this pimciple. It ig that if one body presses against another tho socond resists the aotion of the first with the same force. And also， though it is not so oasy to conceive of $^ { 1 \dagger , }$ if one body acts upon another through any distance, the second reacts upon the first with an equal and oppositely directed force.

Suppose one can exert $^ { \mathfrak { a } }$ given force at will; then,by the second law of motion, the relative masses of bodics can bo measured since they are inversely proportional to the accelcrations which equal forces generate in them When their relativo masses have been found the third law can be tested by permitting the various bodics to act upon one another and measuring their relative accelerations. Newton made several experiments to vorify the law, such as measuring the rebounds from tho impacts of clastic bodios,and observing the accclerations of magnets floating in basins of wator.t The chef dificulty in the experiments arises in elminating forces extcrnal to the systom under considoration,and evidcntly thoy cannot be completely removed. Newton also concluded from a certain course of reasoning that to deny the third law would be to contradiat the first.t

Maoh points out that there is no accurnte moans of measuring \* Trate do Mecanque, vol $\texttt { r } _ { 1 } \texttt { p } 4 5 \texttt { e } ^ { \prime }$ seq. +Prnouma, Scholium to the Laws of Motion.

forces except by tho acceleiations they produco in masses，and theieforo that effectively the leagonng in the preceding paragraph is in a cirele Ho objects also to Newton's definition that mass i8 proportional to the pioduct of the volume and the density of ${ \mathfrak { a } }$ body.IIo prefeis to rely upon experience for the fact that two bodics which act upon cach othei produco oppositely directed accelerations,and io define the relativc values of the masses ag inveisely piopoitional to these accclerations,Experience pioves further lhat if thc ielative masses of two bodics aie determined by thcir inteiactions with a third, the ratio is the same whatovor lhe thild mass may be.In this way,when one body is taken ag the unil of mass, thc masses of all other bodies can be uniquely dcterminedThcse views have much to commcnd them

In lhc Scholum appended to the Laws of Motion Nowton made somc iemarks concerning an important feature of thc thnd law This was first stalcd in a manner in which it could actually be expicsscd n mathcmatical symbols by d'Alembeit in 1742,and has ever sincc been known by his name.\* It is essentially ths: When ${ \mathfrak { a } }$ body is subject to an acceleralion,it may be iegarded as excrtmg $\mathfrak { a }$ forco which 1s cqual and opposile to the foice by which thc acceleration is produced．This may bo consideied as bcing true whcther the force alises fiom anothor body foiming $\pmb { \mathrm { a } }$ systom with the onc under consideration, ol has its source cxterior to tho system. In geneial, in a system of any number of bodies, tho rcsultants of all the apphed forccs aro cqual and opposite to tho rcactions of thc respoctivo bodies.In other words, the smpressed forces and the ieactions,or the expressed forces, foim Bystems which are in equilibrium for cach body and for the whole systom. This makes the whole science of Dynamics,in form,one of Statics, and formulates tho conditions so that they are oxpressible in mathematioal tcrms. This phrasing of the thud law of motion hag been made the starling point for the clegant and very general investgations of Lagrange in the subjoct of Dynamics.t

The primary purpose of fundamental principles in a science is to coordinate tho various phenomena by Btating in what respects their modes of occurring arc common； the value of fundamental principles depends upon the completeness of tho cobrdination of the phenomena,and upon the roadiness with which they lead to the discovory of unknown facts； the characteristics of fundamental prinoiples should be that they are self-consistent, that they are consistent with evcry observed phenomenon,and that they are simple and not redundant

Nowton's laws coordinalc the phenomena of the mechanical sciences in $\clubsuit$ remarkable manner，while their value in making discoveries is witnessed by the brilliant achicvemcnts in the physical sciences in the last two centuries compared to the slow and uncertain advances of all the ancicnts. They have not beon found to be mutually conti adictory,and they are consistent with nearly all the phenomena which have been so fal observed; thoy are conspicuous for their simplicity, but it has bocn claimed by some that they are in certain respects redundant. One naturally wonders whether they are primary and fundamontal laws of nature, even as modified by the piinciple of relativity. In vicw of the past evolution of scientific and philosophical ideas one should bo slow in affirming that any statoment iepi esents ultimate and absolute truth. The fact that soveral othcr sots of fundamental principles have becn made thc bascs of systoms of mechanics， points to the possibility that perhaps some time the Newtonian system,or the Newtonian system as modified by the principle of relalivity,cvcn though it may nol be found to be in erior,will be supplanted by a simpler one cven in elemontary books.

# DDFINITIONS AND GENERAL EQUATIONB

9.Rectilinear Motion, Speed， Velocity. A particlc is in rectlinear motzon when it always lics in tho same straight lino, and when its distance from a point m that lnc varies with the timo. It moves with uniform speed if it passcg ovcr cqual distanccs in equal intcrvals of time, whalever thoir Iongth. Tho speed is representcd by a positive numbcr,and is mcasured by the distanco passcd ovor in $\mathfrak { a }$ unit of $\mathfrak { a }$ timo. Tho velocity of a particle is tho directcd spced with which it moves,and is positivo or negativo accoiding to the direotion of tho motion. Icnce in uniform moton tho velocity is givon by the equation

$$
\ y = \frac { \delta } { \dot { t } }
$$

Since & may be positivo or negativo, $\pmb { \vartheta }$ may bo posilivc or negative, and the speed is the numerical valuo of $\pmb { v }$ Tho samc value of $\mathfrak { V }$ ig obtained whatever interval of time is takon so long as thc corregponding value of s is usod.

The speed and velocity are variable when the particle does not describe equal distances in cqual times,and it is necessary to define in this case what is meant by the speed and velocity at an instant Suppose a particlc passes over the distanco △s im the time $\Delta t ,$ and suppose the inlerval of time $\Delta t$ approaches the limit zero in such a manner that it always contains the instant t. Suppose, further,that for cvery $\Delta t$ the corresponding △s is taken.Then the velocity at the instant $\pmb { \ell }$ 1s defined as

$$
\quad \vartheta = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { \Delta \ell = 0 } \left( \frac { \Delta s } { \Delta t } \right) = \frac { d s } { d t } ,
$$

andthepedise $\frac { d \vartheta } { d \ell }$

Uniform and variable velocity may be defined analytically in the following mannerThe distancc s, counted from $\mathfrak { a }$ fixed point, is considered as $^ { \mathfrak { a } }$ function of the time,and may bo written

$$
\begin{array} { r } { s = \phi ( t ) } \end{array}
$$

Then the velocity may be defined by the equation

$$
\nu = { \frac { d \vartheta } { d t } } = \phi ^ { \prime } ( t ) ,
$$

where $\phi ^ { \prime } ( t )$ is the derivative of $\phi ( t )$ with respect to $\ell _ { \bullet }$ The velocity is said to be constant, or uniform,if $\phi ^ { \prime } ( t )$ does not vary with $\mathbf { \overrightarrow { t } } ;$ or, in other words, f $\phi ( t )$ involves $\pmb { \ell }$ lineally in the form ${ \phi } ( t ) = a t { + } b ,$ where $\pmb { \alpha }$ and $\pmb { b }$ are constants.It is said to be variable if the value of $\phi ^ { \prime } ( t )$ changes with $\pmb { \ell } _ { \pmb { \ell } }$

Some agreement must be made to denote the direction of motion. An arbitrary point on the lino may be taken as the origin and the distances to the right counted as positive,and thoge to the left,negativc.With lhis convention,if the value of s determining the position of tho body increases algebraiaally with the timo the velocity will bo takon positive, if the value of s decreases as the time incrcases the velocity will be taken negative. Thon,when v is given in magnitude and sign,the speed and direction of motion are determined.

$\pmb { \psi 1 0 } .$ Acceleration in Rectilinear Motion. Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity,and may be constant or variable Since the case when it is variable includos that when it is constant,it will be suffoient to consider the former, The definition of acceleration at an instant $\pmb { \ t }$ 1s similar to that for velocity,and is,if the acceleration is denoted by $\pmb { \alpha } _ { \pmb { \mathscr { s } } }$

$$
\alpha = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { \Delta t = 0 } \left( \frac { \Delta v } { \Delta t } \right) = \frac { d v } { d t } .
$$

By means of (2）and (3) it follows that

$$
\alpha = \frac { d } { d t } \left( \frac { d s } { d t } \right) = \frac { d ^ { 2 } s } { d t ^ { 2 } } .
$$

Theie must be an agreement regarding the sign of thc acceleration.When the velocity increases algebiaically as the time incl cases, the acceloration willbe taken positive, when the velocity decreases algebraically as the time increases, thc acceleration will be taken negative

11.Speed and Velocities in Curvilinear Motion. The specd with which a particle moves is the rate al which it describcs $\mathfrak { a }$ gurve. If $\pmb { \upsilon }$ represents the speed m this case,and s thc arc of the curve, then

$$
\vartheta = \left| \frac { d \vartheta } { d t } \right| ,
$$

$\mathrm { w h e r e } \left| \frac { d s } { d t } \right|$ represents te numerical aue f $\frac { d \vartheta } { d t }$ As before, the velocity is the directed speed possessing the propertics of vectorg, and may bo rcpresentcd by a vector \* The vectoi can bo resolvcd uniquely into three componcnts parallel to any thrco coordinate axes；and conversely, the three componenls can bo compounded uniquely into the vector. In other woids, if tho velocily is given, the compononts paralol to any cooidinate axes aro defined, and tho compononts paralol to any non-coplanar coordinate axes define tho velocily It is generally smplest to uso rectangular axeg and to cmploy the components of velocity parallol to thom. Lct $\lambda , ~ \mu , ~ \nu$ represont the angles botwcen tho line of motion and the $\approx , \ : y ,$ and $\pmb { \mathscr { z } }$ -axes respcctivoly. Then

$$
\cos \lambda = \frac { d x } { d s } , \quad \cos \mu = \frac { d y } { d s } , \quad \cos \nu = \frac { d z } { d s } .
$$

Let $v _ { z } , v _ { y } , v _ { z }$ represent the components of velocity along the three axeB.That is,

\*Consult Appells Mecamque vol 1,p.45ele

$$
\left\{ \begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle { \boldsymbol { v } _ { x } = \boldsymbol { v } \cos \lambda = \frac { d s } { d t } \frac { d \boldsymbol { v } } { d s } = \frac { d \boldsymbol { x } } { d t } \mathrm { , } } } \\ { \displaystyle { \boldsymbol { v } _ { y } = \boldsymbol { v } \cos \mu = \frac { d s } { d t } \frac { d \boldsymbol { y } } { d s } = \frac { d \boldsymbol { y } } { d t } \mathrm { , } } } \\ { \displaystyle { \boldsymbol { v } _ { z } = \boldsymbol { v } \cos \nu = \frac { d s } { d t } \frac { d \boldsymbol { z } } { d s } = \frac { d \boldsymbol { z } } { d t } \mathrm { , } } } \end{array} \right.
$$

From theso equations it follows that

$$
\begin{array} { r } { v = \sqrt { \left( \frac { d v } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { d y } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { d z } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } } . } \end{array}
$$

Thcre must be an agreement as to $\clubsuit$ positive and $\mathfrak { a }$ negativo direction along each of the three cooldinalo axes.

12.Acceleration in Curvilinear Motion. As in the case of velocitieg,it is smplest toresolvo the acceleration into component accelerations parallcl to the codidinato axcs,On constructing $\mathfrak { a }$ notation coresponding lo that used in Art.11the following equations result:

\begin{array} { r } { \alpha = \sqrt { \left( \frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 3 } + \left( \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d t ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 3 } + \left( \frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d t ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } } . } \end{array}

This is not, in general, equal to the component of acceleration along the curve; that is, $\tan \frac { d ^ { 2 } \vartheta } { d t ^ { 2 } }$ For,from (8) it follows hat

\frac { d ^ { 2 } \vartheta } { d \ell ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \frac { d z } { d t } \frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \frac { d y } { d t } \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \frac { d z } { d t } \frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d t ^ { 2 } } } { \sqrt { \left( \frac { d x } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { d y } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { d z } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } } } ~ .
$$

Thus,when the components of acceleration are known， the whole acceleration is given by (10)，and the acceleration along the curve by (11).The fact that the two aic diffoient, m general, may cause some surpiisc at fist thought But the mattcr becomes clear if $\mathfrak { a }$ body moving m & circle with conslant spccd is considered.The acceleialion along the cuive is zcro bccause the speed is supposed not to change,but the accclciation is not zero because the body does not move in & staight linc

15. Application to a Particle Moving in a Circle with Uniform Speed. Suppose the particle moves with uniform spced in a cirelo around the origin as center;it IB required to determine the com-

![](images/2589f0735a1c2dd39f617e36802e90bd8287c91acd6e5503463373f0a44f8246.jpg)  
Fg 2.

ponents of velocity and acceleration parailel to the $\pmb { x }$ and y-axes, and paralel and perpendioular to the radius. Let $\pmb { R }$ represent the radius of the cirole；then

$$
{ \mathfrak { x } } = R \cos \theta , \qquad { \mathfrak { y } } = R \sin \theta .
$$

Since the speed is uniform the angle $\pmb \theta$ is proportional to the time, or $\theta = c t _ { y }$ The cobrdinates become

$$
{ \mathfrak { x } } = R \cos { ( c t ) } , \qquad { \mathfrak { y } } = R \sin { ( c t ) } .
$$

Since $\frac { d \theta } { d t } = c$ and $\frac { d R } { d t } = 0 ,$ tho components of velocity parallel to the $\pmb { \mathscr { D } }$ and $\pmb { y }$ -axes are found from (13) to be

$$
\vartheta _ { z } = - \ R c \sin { ( c t ) } , \qquad \vartheta _ { y } = R c \cos { ( c t ) } .
$$

From (16) it is found that

$$
\vartheta _ { r } = 0 , \qquad \vartheta _ { \theta } = \mathcal { R } \mathsf { c } .
$$

The components of acceleration paralel to the $\pmb { \mathscr { B } }$ and y-axesj which are given by (17), are

$$
\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \alpha _ { z } = - R c ^ { 2 } \cos { ( c t ) } , } \\ { \alpha _ { y } = - R c ^ { 2 } \sin { ( c t ) } . } \end{array} \right.
$$

And from (19) it is found that

$$
\alpha _ { r } = - \ R c ^ { 2 } , \alpha _ { \theta } = 0 .
$$

It will be observed that,although the specd is uniform in this case,the velocity with respect to fixcd axcs is not constant,and the acceleation is not zeroIf it is assumed that an exterior force is the only cause of the change of motion,or of acceleration of $^ { \mathfrak { a } }$ particle, then it follows lhal a paitiele cannot move in a oircle with uniform speed unless it is subject to some forco. It follows also from (26)and the second law of motion that the force continually acts in a line which passes through the conter of the circle.

16.The Areal Velocity. \frac { d A } { d t } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } r ^ { 2 } \frac { d \theta } { d t }

as the expression for the areal velocity. On changing to rec angular coordinates by the substitution

$$

18. Center of Mass of n Equal Particles. The center of mass of a system of equal particles will be defincd as that point whose distance from any plane is equal to the average distance of all of the particles from that plane This must be true then for the three reference plancgLet (xiV1, z1),(xz,yz, 2),etc,repiesent the rectangular cobrdinates of the various particles, and ,g, the rectangular cobrdinates of their center of mass；then by the definition

$$
\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \bar { \boldsymbol { w } } = \displaystyle \frac { \boldsymbol { x } _ { 1 } + \boldsymbol { x } _ { 2 } + \cdot \mathrm {  ~ \nabla ~ } + \boldsymbol { x } _ { n } } { n } = \frac { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \boldsymbol { x } _ { i } } { n } , } \\ { \bar { \boldsymbol { w } } = \displaystyle \frac { \boldsymbol { y } _ { 1 } + \boldsymbol { y } _ { 2 } + \cdot \mathrm {  ~ \nabla ~ } \cdot + \boldsymbol { y } _ { n } } { n } = \frac { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \boldsymbol { y } _ { i } } { n } , } \\ { \bar { \boldsymbol { u } } = \displaystyle \frac { \boldsymbol { z } _ { 1 } + \boldsymbol { z } _ { 2 } + \cdot \mathrm {  ~ \nabla ~ } \cdot + \boldsymbol { z } _ { n } } { n } = \frac { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \boldsymbol { z } _ { i } } { n } . } \end{array} \right.
$$

Suppose the mags of each partiole ig $m ,$ and let $\pmb { M }$ represent the mass of the whole system，or $M = n m$ On multiplying the numerators and denominators by m, equations (32) become

$$
\left\{ \begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \bar { x } = \frac { m \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { 1 } } { n m } = \frac { \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m x _ { i } } { \displaystyle M } , } \\ { \displaystyle \bar { y } = \frac { m \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { 1 } } { n m } = \frac { \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m y _ { i } } { \displaystyle M } , } \\ { \displaystyle \bar { z } = \frac { m \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } z _ { i } } { \displaystyle n m } = \frac { \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m z _ { i } } { \displaystyle M } } \end{array} \right.
$$

Il remains to show that the distance from the point $( { \bar { x } } , \ { \bar { y } } , \ { \bar { z } } )$ to any other plane is also the avcrage distance of the particles from tho plano. The oquation of any othcr plane is

$$
a x + b y + c z + d = 0 .
$$

The distance of the point $( ☉ , \mathfrak { F } , \breve { z } )$ from this plane 1s

$$
\overrightarrow { d } = \frac { q \overrightarrow { x } + b \overrightarrow { y } + c \overrightarrow { z } + d } { \sqrt { a ^ { 2 } + b ^ { 2 } + c ^ { 2 } } } ,
$$

and similarly, the distance of the point (x, V, z） fom the same plane is

$$
d _ { * } = \frac { a _ { 1 } + b y _ { 4 } + c z _ { 1 } + d } { \sqrt { a ^ { 2 } + b ^ { 2 } + c ^ { 2 } } } .
$$

It follows from equations (32),(34),and (35) that

$$
\widetilde { d } = \frac { a \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } { x _ { i } + b \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } { y _ { i } + c \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } { z _ { i } + n d } } } } { n \sqrt { a ^ { 2 } + b ^ { 2 } + c ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } { d _ { i } } } { n } .
$$

Therefore the point (ε,D,) defined by (32)satisfics the definition of center of mass with respcct to all plancs.

19.Center of Mass of Unequal Particles. There are two cases, (a) that in which the masses are commensurablc,and (b) that im which the masses are incommensurable

(a) Select a unit m in terms of which all thc n masscs can be expressed integiallySuppose the first mass is pim, thc second p2m,ctc,and let pim = m, pm = mz, etc. The systcm may bo thought of as made up of p1 + pz+ .. particlcs each of mass m, and consequently,by Art.18,

$$
\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \displaystyle \bar { z } = \frac { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m p _ { i } \bar { z } _ { i } } { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m p _ { i } } = \frac { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } \bar { z } _ { i } } { \lambda I } , } \\ { \displaystyle \bar { y } = \frac { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m p _ { i } \bar { y } _ { i } } { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m p _ { i } } = \frac { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } \bar { y } _ { i } } { \lambda I } , } \\ { \displaystyle \bar { z } = \frac { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m p _ { i } \bar { z } _ { i } } { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m p _ { i } } = \frac { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } \bar { z } _ { i } } { \lambda I } , } \end{array} \right.
$$

(b) Select an arbitrary unit m smallcr than any one of the n masses. Take as & new unit any submultiplp of m and the remainders will remain the same, or be diminished, depending on their magnitudes. Tho submultiple of m can be taken so small that overy remainder is smaller than any assigned quantity. Equations (36) contmually hold wheie the m, are the masses of the bodies minus the remaindeisAs the submultiples of m appioach zolo as a limit, the sum of the 1emaindcis appioaches ze1o as a limit,and the cxpicssions (36) appioach as limits the cxpressions im which the m, aie tho actual masscs of tho particles Thciofore im all cascs cquations (36) give a point which satisfies the defimtion of conter of mags.

The fact that if thc defimtion of center of mass is fulflled for the tlueo ieference plancs,it is also,fulfilled fol every other plane can oasily be pioved without recouise to the general formula for thc distance from any point to any plano.It is to be obseived that the yz-planc, fol cxamplc,may be biought mto any position whatevcr by a change of oiigm and a succession of rotalions of the cobidmate systcm aiound tho various axcsIt will be necessaiy to show, then, that equalions (36) aic not changed in foim (1) by a change of origin,and (2) by a iotation around onc of the axcs.

(1） Transfer the origin along the x-axis through the distance a Thc substitution which accomplishes the lransfer is e = x'+a, and tho firsl cqualion of (3G) becomes

$$
\tilde { x } ^ { \prime } + a = \frac { \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } ( x _ { i } ^ { \prime } + a ) } { M } = \frac { \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } x _ { i } ^ { \prime } } { M } + \frac { \displaystyle a \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } } { M } ;
$$

whence

$$
\tilde { \mathfrak { X } } ^ { \prime } \equiv \frac { \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } \mathfrak { X } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } } { \displaystyle \mathcal { M } } ,
$$

which hag the same form as beforo.

(2) Rotato tho α and y-axes around the z-axis through the angle 0. The substitution which accomplishes the rotation is

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \int _ { \mathcal { Y } } \subset \ d { x } ^ { \prime } \cos \theta - \ d { y } ^ { \prime } \sin \theta , } \\ { \ d { y } = \ d { x } ^ { \prime } \sin \theta + \ d { y } ^ { \prime } \cos \theta . } \end{array}
$$

Tho first two cquations of (36) becomo by this transformation

$$
\left\{ \begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \hat { \bar { \boldsymbol { \alpha } } } ^ { \prime } ~ \cos \theta - \bar { \boldsymbol { y } } ^ { \prime } ~ \sin \theta = \cos \theta ~ { \frac { \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } \alpha _ { i } ^ { \prime } } { \displaystyle { M } } } - \sin \theta ~ { \frac { \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } \mu _ { i } ^ { \prime } } { \displaystyle { M } } } } , } \\ { { \displaystyle \bar { \boldsymbol { \alpha } } ^ { \prime } ~ \sin \theta + \bar { \boldsymbol { y } } ^ { \prime } ~ \cos \theta = \sin \theta ~ { \frac { \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } \alpha _ { i } ^ { \prime } } { \displaystyle { M } } } + \cos \theta ~ { \frac { \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } \mu _ { i } ^ { \prime } } { \displaystyle { M } } } } . } \end{array} \right.
$$

On solving these equations it is found that

$$
\tilde { x } ^ { \prime } = \frac { \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } x _ { i } ^ { \prime } } { M } , ~ \bar { y } ^ { \prime } = \frac { \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } y _ { i } ^ { \prime } } { M } \cdot
$$

Therefore the point $( \bar { x } , \ \bar { y } , \ \bar { z } )$ satisfics tho definition of contcr of mass with respect to every plane

20.The Center of Gravity. Tho mcmbers of & syslem of particles which are neai togother at the surfacc of tho enrth nra subjeet to forces downwaid which are sensibly parallel and proportional to their respective masses.Tha werghl,or gravily,of n particle will be defined as thc intensiy of the vertical force $f _ { f }$ which is the product of the mass m of the particlo and itg aocelern tion g. The center of gravity of tho syslem will bo dofincd ng lle point such that, if the members of the system woro rigidly con\* nected and the sum of all the forcces wcro applied at this polnty then the effect on the motion of tho systom would bo tho snma that of the original forces for all oriontations of the systom.

![](images/dc60a40169c9705d0787f274ae377213de8325f0ca2f08826fed9ec87baa771f.jpg)

It will now be shown that the center of gravity coincidea wlth the center of mass, Considcr two parallol forcesf and fa nollng upon the rigid system $M \mathfrak { a t }$ the points ${ \pmb P _ { 1 } }$ and $\pmb { \mathscr { P } _ { 2 } }$ Rcsolvo thes two forces into the componentsfand g,and fand fa rospoctlvaly The components f,being equal and opposito，destroy cach otliar Then the components gi and g may be regarded as ncting at A Resolve them again so that the oppositely direoted componont shall be equalandlieinalneparllel to $\mathcal { P } _ { 1 } \mathcal { P } _ { 2 }$ then the other compononts willieinthe same line $\pmb { A B }$ which isparallel to the dnectaon of the oliginal forces fi and f, and will bo cqual respectively to ${ f } _ { 1 }$ and $f _ { 2 }$ Theicfoic tho esultant of $f _ { 1 }$ and $\pmb { f _ { 2 } }$ 1s equal to $f _ { 1 } + f _ { 2 }$ inmagnitude and direction.It is found from similar tuangles that

$$
\frac { f _ { 1 } } { f } = \frac { A B } { P _ { 1 } B } , \frac { f _ { 2 } } { f } = \frac { A B } { P _ { 2 } B } ;
$$

whenco,by division,

$$
\frac { \hat { f _ { 1 } } } { \hat { f _ { 2 } } } = \frac { \hat { P _ { 2 } } \vec { B } } { \hat { P _ { 1 } } \vec { B } } = \frac { \hat { x _ { 2 } } } { \hat { x } } \frac { - \vec { x } } { - \hat { x _ { 1 } } } .
$$

Tho solution for $\Re$ gives

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \ddot { \mathfrak { x } } = \frac { f _ { 1 } \mathfrak { x } _ { 1 } + f _ { 2 } \mathfrak { x } _ { 2 } } { f _ { 1 } + f _ { 2 } } . } \end{array}
$$

If the resultant of thcso two foices bo united with a third force fs, the pomt whcic lhcir sum may bo applied with the same effects is found m n similar manner to be given by

$$
\tilde { x } = \frac { \int _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } + \int _ { 2 } x _ { 2 } + \int _ { 1 } x _ { 3 } } { \int _ { 1 } + \int _ { 2 } + \int _ { 3 } } ,
$$

and so on for nny number of forccs Similar equations are true for parallel forecs acting in any othor direction.

Suppose thero aro n particlcs mt subject lo n paralll forccs fi due io tho attraction of tho carth The coordmates of their contol of gravity with respcct to lho origin aro given by

$$
{ \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { { \bar { x } } = { \frac { \displaystyle { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } } f _ { i } { \bar { x } } _ { i } } { \displaystyle { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } } f _ { i } } } = { \frac { \displaystyle { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } } \jmath \sigma m _ { i } x _ { i } } { \displaystyle { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } } \jmath \sigma m _ { i } } } = { \frac { \displaystyle { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } x _ { i } } } { \displaystyle { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } } \jmath } } , } \\ { { \bar { y } } = { \frac { \displaystyle { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } y _ { i } } } { \displaystyle { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } } } } , } \\ { { \bar { z } } = { \frac { \displaystyle { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } z _ { i } } } { \displaystyle { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \jmath } } } . } \end{array} \right. }
$$

Tho centor of gravity is thus scen to bo coincident with the center of mass； novortheloss this would not in genoral bo true if the sygtem wero not in such $\pmb { \mathscr { a } }$ position that tho accclorations to whioh its various members are subjeet were both equal and parnllel. Euler (1707-1783)proposed the designatonof center ofinerta for the center of mass.

21. Center of Mass of a Continuous Body. As the partiolcs of a system become more and moie numerous and noaror togther it approaches as a lmit & continuous body. In tho caso of tho ordinary bodies of mechanics the particles are innumerable and indistinguishably close together；onthis account such bodios aro treated as continuous masses. For contimuous masscs, thorofore, the limits of expressions (37),as m,approaches zero,must bo takenAt the limit m becomes dm and tho sum bocomcs tho dofi\~ nite integral. The equations which give the center of masg aro therefore

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \left\{ \bar { x } = \frac { f x d m } { \bar { f } d m } , \right. } \\ { \bar { y } = \frac { \bar { f } y \bar { d } m } { \bar { f } d m } , } \\ { \bar { z } = \frac { \bar { f } z d m } { \bar { f } d m } , } \end{array}
$$

where the integrals are to be extended throughout the whole body.

When the body is homogencous the density is thequotiontof any portion of the mass dividcd by its volumc.When the body is not homogeneous the mean density is tho quoticnt of tho wholc mass divided by the whole volume. The density at any point tg the lmit of the mean density of a volume including tho point in question when this volume approachcs zcro as a limit.If tho. density is repregented by g,the element of mass is, when exprosged in rectangular coordinates,

$$
d m = \sigma d x d y d z .
$$

Then equations (38) become

$$
\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \bar { x } = \frac { \iiint \sigma ; d x d y d z } { \iiint f d x d y d z } , } \\ { \bar { y } = \frac { \iiint \sigma y d x d y d z } { \iiint \sigma d x d y d z } , } \\ { \bar { z } = \frac { \iiint \sigma z d x d y d z } { \iiint \sigma d x d y d z } . } \end{array} \right.
$$

The limits of the integrals depend upon the shape of the body, andg must be expressed as a funetion of tho codrdinates.

In certain probloms the mtegrations are performed more simply if polar cooidinatcs are employed， The element of mass when oxpressed in polar coordinates is

$$

25.The Two Divisions of the History. The history of tho dovelopment of Celcstial Mcchanics is naluially divided into two distincl parts. The one is concerncd with the piogress of knowlcdgo about the puroly formal aspcct of thc universc, the natural divisions of timo,the configuralions of the constellations,and tho detormination of tho paths and periods of the planels in thoir motions;the other treats of thc cffoits at,and the success in,atlaining corrcct ideas iegaiding the physical aspects of natural phcnomcna, thc fundamental pioperties of foicc,malloi,space,and time,and, in particular, the way n which they aie relatcd It is true that these two lines in tho development of asticnomical science have nol always been kopt distinel by those who havo cultivated thcm；on the conliary，they have often been so intmatcly associatcd that thc spcculationg in thc latlei havo influcnced umduly the conelusions in the foimeiWhilc it is clcar thal the two kinds of invcstigation should be kept dislmcl n the imind of the mvestigaloi,il is equally cleai that lhoy should be constantly cmployed as checks upon cach other.Tho objcct of the next two articles willbe to liace,mas few woids as possiblo thc development of thcsc two lincs of progrcss of the scicnco of Celestial Mechanics from the timcs of the caily Grcck Philosophors to the timc when Nowton applied his tianscendenl gonius lo tho analysis of tho clements mvolved,and to thel synthcsis into one of thc sublmcst pioducts of the human mind

26.Formal Astronomy．Thc first division 1s concerncd with phenomena altogethei apail fiom thcir causes,and will be tcrmod Foimal AslionomyThe day,the monlh, and tho ycar aro such obvious natural divisions of time that thoy must have bcen noticcd by the mosl primilivo pcoplcsBul the determination of the iclations ainong thesc peiiodg requned somctling of tho soientific spirit necssry for careful obscrvations; ${ \bf y 6 6 } ,$ in the very dawn of Chaldean and Egyptian history they appcai to hnve been known wilh a considcrable degicc of accuiacy. Tho iccords left by thcso pcoples of thcir carhcr civilizalions ar so mcagor that lilc is known with certainty iegaidng lholr scientific achicvomentsThe authentic history of Astonomy actually begins with tho Grecks, who,doriving thch fist knowledgo and mspiration from tho Egyplians, pusucd tle subject with the enthusinsm and acuteness which was charactelstic of tho Greek iace

Thales (640-546 B.c.),of Miletus,went to Egypt for instructon,and on his roturn founded the Ionian School of Astonony and Philosophy， Some idca of the advancomont made by the Egyptians can be gatheicd from the facl that hc taught the sphericity of the earth,the obliquity of the cliptic,the causes of eclipses,and, according to IIerodotus, picdiclcd the eclipso of the sun of 58b B.o. According to Laerlius he was tho first to dctormino the longth of tho ycar. It is fair to assume that he borrowed much of his mformation fiom Egypt, though thc basis for prodicting eclipses icsts on the peiiod of 658b days,known as the saros，discovered by tho ChaldacansAflcl the lapse of this period elipses.of tho sun and moon rccut under almost identical cu cumstances except that they auo displaccd about 120o westward on thc caith.

Anaximander (61l-545 B c）,a fiend and probably a pupil of Thales,constiucted geogiaphical maps,and is ciedited wilh having mvonted the gnomon,

Pythagoras (aboul 569-470 B.c.) traveled widely in Egypt and Chaldea, ponetrating Asta cvcn to thc banks of the Ganges. On his icturn he went to Sicily and foundcd a School of Astronomy and Phlosophy. Ho taught that tho carth both iolalcs and revolves,and that the comots as well as tho plancls inove in orbits alound thc sun He is cicdited with being tho first to maimtan that thc same planel, Venus, is both cvcning and moining stal at diffor ont times.

Mcton (aboul 405-385 B.g）broughl to thc notice of tho learned mcn of Iellas the cyclo of 19 years, ncarly cquallng 235 synodie months, which has smcc bcen known as the Motonc oycle. After the lapse of this period lhe phases of the moon iecul on the same days of the ycai,and almost at tho samo timo of day The still moic accurate Calhpic cycle consists of four Motonic cycles, less one day

Aiistollo (384-322 B c.） mainlained the theoiy of the globular form of tho carlh and supported il with many of tho arguments which aro used al tho prcsont timo.

Aristaichus (310-25o B.c.） wioto an impolLant work entitled Magntudes and Drstances. In it he caleulated from the timc at which tho carlh is in quadlraiure as scon from thc moon thal the latter is aboul onc-ninotconlh as dislnnt from the carth as the sun, The time m qucstion is determined by observing whon the moon is at tho firsi quailer. Tho piactioal diffieully of dctorininmg cxaetly whon tho moon has any parlicular phase is the only thing that pevcnls the method, which 1s theorelically sound,from boing entiroly successful.

Eralosthencs (275-194 B.c.） mado & cataloguc of 476 of the brightest stars,and is famous for having lotomincd tho size of tho earth from the measurement of tho differonce in latitudo and the distanco apart of Syenc,in Uppor Egypl, and Alexandiia.

Hipparchus (（about 190-120 B.o.)，a native of Bithynia, who observed at Rhodes and possibly at Alexandria, was the greatest astronomer of antiquity He addcd to zeal and skill as an obsorver the accomplshments of the mathematician Following Eucld (about 330-275 B.c）at Alexandria,he devclopcd tho important seience of Spherical Trgonometiy. Ie located placcs on the earth by their Longitudes and Latitudes,and the stars by their Right Ascensions and Declnations. IIe was led by tho appearance of a temporary star to make a catalogue of 1o8o fixcd stars. He measuicd the length of the tropical year, thc length of the month from eclipses, the motion of the moon's nodcs and that of her apogce; he was the author of the first solar tables; ho discovered tho precession of the equinoxes, and made extensivo observations of the planets. The works of Hipparchus are known only indireotly, his own writingg having been lost at the time of the destruotion cf the grent Alexandrian libiary by tho Saracens under Omar, in $\Updownarrow 4 0 \phantom { \Delta } _ { \pm \mathbf { b } } .$

Ptolemy $( 1 0 0 - 1 7 0 \wedge . \mathfrak { D } . )$ ）carried forward the work of Ilipparchus faithfully and left the Almagest as the monument of his labors. Fortunately il has come down to modern times intact and conlaing much information of great value Ptolemy's grcatcst discovery is the eveotion of the moon's motion, which ho dotecled by following the moon during the wholo month, instead of confining his attention to certain phascs as previous obscrvers had dono. He discovered refraction, but is particularly famous foi the systom of ccentrics and opicycles which ho devoloped to cxplain tho apparont motions of the planets.

A stationary period followed Ptolemy during which scienco wag not cultivatcd by any pcoplo cxcept the Aiabs,who wcre imitators and commcntators ralhor moie than original mvcstigatorg. In the Ninth Century tho gieatcst Arabian astronomer,Albategniug (850-929), flourishcd,and $^ \mathfrak { a }$ moro acourale measuremcnt of the arc of a mcridian than had before that timc bcen executed wag garricd out by him in tho plain of Singiar, in Mcsopotamia. In tho Tenth Contury Al-Sufi made a cataloguo of stars bascd on his own observations. Another cataloguc was made by thc direotion of Ulugh Be1gh (1393-1449),at Samarkand,in 1433.At this time Arabian asti onomy practically coased to cxist.

Aslionomy bcgan to rovivo in Europe towaid tho end of lho Fifteenth Century m the labors of Pourbach (1423-1461), Waltherus (1430-1504)，and Regiomontanus (1436-1476).It was given & great impetus by the celobratcd Gorman astronomor

Copernicus (1473-1543)，and has been pursued with increasing zeal to the piesent timeCopernicus published his masterpiece, De Revolulrontbus Orbrum Coelestum,in 1543,m which he gavc to the world thc heliocentic theory of thc solar system The systcm of Copcinicus was iojected by Tycho Brahe (1546-1601), who advanced a theoiy of his own,bccause he could not observe any parallax in the fixed stais. Tyeho was of Norwegian birth, but did much of his aslronomical work m Denmaik under the patronage of Kmg Fredeiick After tho death of Frederick he moved to Prague whcro ho was suppoited the remainder of hig hfc by a lbeal pcnsion from Rudolph II. IIo was an indcfatigable and most painstaking observer, and made very impoitant contributions to Astionomy. In his laler yearg Tycho Brahe had Kepler (1571-1630) for his disciple and assistant,and it was by discussing the obsei vations of Tycho Brahe tlat Kepler was cnabled,m less than twenty yeais after the death of his piecoptor, to announce the thicc laws of planctary motion. It was fiom these laws that Ncwlon (1642-1727) deiivcd tho law of gravitation.

Galilco (1564-1642),an Itahan aslionomer,a contcmpoiary of Koplei,and & man of groater genius and greater fame, fist applied thc telcscope to celestial objccts.IIe discovoicd foul satelltcs rcvolvmg aiound Jupiter， tho imgs of Saturn, and spols on the sun. IIc,hke Kopler,was an ardont supportor of the heliocentric thcory.

27. Dynamical Astronomy. By Dynamical Astronomy will be mcant tho connccting of mechanical and physical causos with obsei ved phcnoinena. Formal Astronomy 18 so ancient that it is not possble to go back to its origin,Dynamical Astronomy,on tho othei hand,did nol bogin until aftci the time of Aristollo,and thon ical advances wero mado at only very rare intervals.

Archimedcs (287-212 B c），of Syracuso,is the authoi of the first sound idoas regarding mechanical laws. IIe stated corioctlly the prmeiples of the lovel and thc meaning of the centcr of giavity of a body. The form and goneralty of his treaimenl wcro improved by Leonardo da Vinci (1452-1b19) in his invcstigations of statical moments. The whole subjcct of Statics of a rigid body involves only thc application of tho pioper mathomatics to these principles.

It is a remarkablo fact thal no single important advance was made in the discovory of mcchanical laws for ncarly lwo thousand ycars after Archimedes,or until tho time of Stovinus (1548-1620).

who was the first,in 1586,toinvestigate the mechanics of the inclned plane,and of Galileo (1564-1642)，whomade the fst important advance In Kinetics.Thus, the meehanical pimciples involved in the motions of bodies were not discoveicd until ielatively modern times.The fundamental ciiol in tho speculations of most of the investigators was that thoy supposcd thal it reqmed a continually acting force to keop a body in motionThey thought it was natural for a body to have a position iather than a stale of motionThis is the oppositc of the law of mertia (Newton's firat law)，This law was discovercd by Galileo quitc incidentally m the study of the motion of bodics shdng down an mclned plano and out on a horizontal surface Galilco look as his fundamental principle that the change of velocity，ol acceoiation,is determined by the foices which act upon the bodyThis contains nearly all of Newton's first two laws.Galieo applcd his principlcg with complete suceessto the discovery of thc laws of falling bodics, and of the motion of projcctilesThe value of his discoveries is such that he is justly considered to be the founder of Dynamicg. He was the first to employ the pendulum fol thc mcasuiement of time

Huyghens (1629-1695),a Dutch mathematician and scicntist, pubhshed his Horologrum Oscillatorium in 167b, contaiming thc theory of the determination of the intensity of the eailh's gravity fiom pendulum cxperiments,the theoiy of the center of oscil lation，the theory of evolutes,and tho lheoiy of tho cycloidal pendulum.\*:

Newton (1642-1727) completcd thc foimulation of thc fundamental principles of Mechanics, and applcd them with unparalleled success in thc solution of mcchanical and aslionomicnl problemg. He employed Geomotry with such skill that his work has scarcely been added to by the uso of his molhods lo thc present day,

After Newton's timo, mathcmalicians soon luuned to ihe moro general and powerful mcthods of analysisThc subject of Analytical Mechanics was foundcd byEulor (1707-1783) in his work, Mechamca sive Motus Scienta (Pelersburg, 173o)；il was improvecl by Maclauim (1698-1746) in his wolk,A Complete Syslem of Fluxions (Edinbugh,1742),and was highly perfocted by Lagrango (1736-18i3) in his Mecanique Analytzque (Paiis，1788) Thc Mecanque Celeste of Laplaco (1740-1827) put Celestial Mochanicg ona correspondingly high plane.

where Ci, Ca, Ci',and c' are the constants of integration.

36.Application to the Escape of Atmospheres. The kinetic theory of gases is that the volumcs and prcssures of gages arc maintained by the mutual impacts of the mdividual moleculcs, which are, on the averagc, in a stato of veiy rapid motionThe rarity of the eaith's atmosphcrc and thc fact that the prcssuro is about fifteen pounds to the squaro inch, sorve to give somo iden of the high speed with which thc molccules movo and of thc grcat frequency of their impacts. The different moleculcs do not all move with the game speed in any given gas under fixcd conditions ; but the number of those which havc a rato of motion diffcrcnt from the mean of the squares bccomcs lcss very rapidly as tho differences increase. Thcoretically,in all gascs tho rango of tho valucs of the velooities is from zero to infinity,although tho extremo cases occur at infinitely rare intervals compared to the otherg. Under constant pressure the velocities are dircctly proportional to thc square root of the absolute temporaturo,and invorsoly proportional to the squarc 1oot of tho molcoular wcight.

Since in all gascs all volocities cxist， some of lho molccules of the gaseous envclopes of tho heavonly bodies must bc moving with velocitics grcater than tho velocity from snfinaty,as defincd in Art.35If the molecules aro ncar tho upper lmits of tho atmosphere,and starl away from tho body lo which they belong, thoy may miss collsions with othcr moloculcs and oscapo nevor to return\*Simce thc kinelic theory of gascs is supporied by vory strong obscrvational ovidence, and sinco if it is truo it is probable that some molecules movo with volocities greater than tho velooity from infimty,it is piobablc that the atmospheres of all colcstial bodies are being depleted by this piocess,but m most cascs it is an excessivcly slow onc, and is compcnsatcd, to somc extent at least,by thc accretion of metcoric mattcr and atmospheric molecules fiom olher bodiesIn thc upper regions of the gaseous envelopes,from which alone tho molcculcs cscapc,the temperalures arc low,at lcast for planetary bodics liko thc carth,and high velocilics arc of raro occuicncc. If the mcan squaio velocity werc as grcat as, or cxccedcd, tho volocily from imfinily the deplction would be & relatively iapid pioccssIn any case thc clements and compounds with low molecular woights would be lost most rapidly,and thus certain ones might ficely cscape and olhcis be largely ictained

The manner in which the velocity fiom infinity with rcspcct to the surfaco of an attacling sphcio varies with its mass and radius will now bo imvcstigated. 40.Work and Energy. When $\mathfrak { a }$ force moves a particle against any resistance it is said to do workThe amount of tho work is piopoitional to the product of thc resistunce and the distance thiough which the pau ticle is moved In the case of $\mathfrak { a }$ free particle the iesistance comcs cntnely fom the imertia of tho mass,if there Is frclion this is also rcsistance

Ener gy is the powei of doing woik If a given amount of work 1s done upon a paiticle fiee lo move, the pai licle acquncs a motion that will cnablc it to do cxaclly the samo amount of work The eneigy of motion is callcd hnetac energy. If thc particle is retaided by friction pail of the original work cxpended will be used m overcoming the fuclion, and tho pau ticlo will be capable of doing only as much work as has been donc in giving it motionUntil about 1850 it was generally supposed thal work donc in overcoming fiiction is partly,or perhaps ontiicly,lostIn othcr words,itwas belicved that thc total amounl of energy in an isolatcd systcm might continually dccicase.It was obscrvcd， howevci，that friction generatcs heat, sound, light,and cleclricity,depending upon the circumstances,and that thcso mamfcstations of energy are of the same nature as tho origmal, but in $\mathfrak { a }$ diffcrent form.It was then proved that thesc modified forms of cnergy aie in every caso quantilatively cquivalenl to the wastc of that originally consideied． Tho brilliant exporiments of Joule and othcrs,made in the middle of tho ninetecnth cenluiy,havc establshed with gicat certainty thc fact lhat tho total amount of cncrgy remains the samo whatever changes it may undeigo. This principle, known as the conser valion of energy，when stalcd ns holdimg throughout thc univcrse, is ono of thc mosl fai-reaching generallzations that has been made in the nalural scienccs in $\mathfrak { a }$ hundred ycars.\*

41.Computation of Work. Tho amount of work done by $\mathfrak { a }$ Newtonian force in moving a frce particle any distance will now be computcd Lct m cqual the mass of the parlicle moved, and ${ \mathit { h } } ^ { 2 }$ a constant deponding upon the mass of thc attracting body and the unils cmployed. The iight membci is the foice to which the pai ticle is subject By Newton's third law it is numerically equal to tho reaction, 0l the resistance due to inertia. Then the woik done in movmg the particle through the clement of distance ds is

$$

$$

The woik done in movmg the paiticlc though the interval from 8o to s1is found by taking the definite integral of this cxpiession betwcen the lmits &o and st On peifoiming the intcgiations and substituting the lmits,it is found that

\frac { m } { 2 } \left( \frac { d s _ { 1 } } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } - \frac { m } { 2 } \left( \frac { d s _ { 0 } } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } = \mathrm { \mathit { k } } ^ { 2 } m \left( \frac { 1 } { s _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { s _ { 0 } } \right)
$$

Suppose thc initial velocity is zoro,then the kinctic energy cquals the woik W done upon the partiele,and

$$
, W = { \frac { m } { 2 } } \left( { \frac { d s _ { 1 } } { d \ell } } \right) ^ { 2 } = k ^ { 2 } m \left( { \frac { 1 } { s _ { 1 } } } - { \frac { 1 } { s _ { 0 } } } \right)
$$

By hypothesis, the particle has no kinetie energy on thc starl, and theiefore tho power of doing work cquals thc product of one half the mass and the square of the velocity. If the particle falls from infinity,so is infinitc,and the formula for the kinetic energy becomes

$$
\frac { m } { 2 } \left( \frac { d s _ { 1 } } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } = \frac { h ^ { 2 } m } { s _ { 1 } }
$$

If the particle is stopped by striking a body whcn it rcaches tho point si, its kinetic energy is changed into some other form of eneigy such as heatIt has been found by experiment that a body weighing one kilogram falling 425 meters\* in the vicinity of the earth's surface, under the infuence of the earth's attraction, generates enough heat to raise the temperature of one kilogram of water one degrce Centigrade. This quantity of heat is callcd the calory t The amount of heat generated is pioportional to thc product of the square of the velocity and the mass of the moving particle. Then， letting Q represent the number of calories，it follows that

$$
Q = C m \upsilon ^ { 2 } .
$$

42.The Temperature of Meteors. The inercase of temperature of a body,whcn the propor units are employcd,is cqual to the numbor of calouics of hcat aequned dhvided by the product of the mass and tho specific heat of the substance. Suppose a meteor whoso spccifia hoat is umity (in faot it would probably be much less than unity) should stike tho oarth with any given velocity,it 1s requiicd lo compulo ils increase of temperature if it took up all the heat gencialed. The specific heat has beon takcn so that the mcroase of lomperatuno is numeiically equal to the number of calories gcnoralcd por unit mass. Meleois usually strike the carth with a velocity of about 25 miles,or 40,233 meters,per second. On substituling 40,233 for v and unity for min (45),it is found that T = Q =194,i34,the numbor of calories generated pcr unt mass,or tho numbcr of degrces through which the temperatuie of the meteor would be raised. As a mattor of fact a large part of tho hcat wouid bc impaited to tho atmosphere； but the quantity of hoat geneiatcd is so cnormous that it could not be expected that any but tho largest metcors would last long enough to reach the earth's surfaco

A motcor fallmg into the sun from an infinite distance would stike its suiface, as has becn seen in Arl 36,with a vclocity of about 384 miles per sccond The heat gonelated would be thel( fore (4)²,or 236, tiines as great as that pioduced in stiiking tho eaithThus it follows that a kilogram would geneiale,m fallng into the sun,45,815,624 calortes.

43.The Meteoric Theory of the Sun's Heat. Whcn it is remembeied what an enoimous numbei of meteo1s (estimatcd by H A Newton\* as being as many as 8,0oo,00o daily） slrike thc earth,it is easily conceivable that cnough stiiko the sun to maintain its temperature Indeed, this has bccn advanced as a thcory to account for the replenishment of thc vasl amount of hcat which the sun iadiates Theie can be no qucstion of its qualitativo colrectness,and it only lemains to camine it quantilativelv.

Let it be assumed that the sun iadiates hcat cqually m evciy direction,and that meteors fall upon it in equal numbeis fiom every direction Under this assumption,the amount of hcat racliated by any portion of the surface will equal that generated by thc impact of meteors upon that portion The amount of heat received by the earth will be to tho whole amount radiatcd fiom the sun as the surface which the carth subtends as secn fiom tho sun is to the surface of the whole spheie whoso radius is thc distance from the earth to the sun. The portion of the sun's surface which is within the cone whose base is the carth and verlex the center of the sun,is to the whole surface of the sun as the surface subtended by the eaith is to the surface of the whole sphere whose iadius is the distance to the sun Thercfore,lho eaith ieceives as much heat as is radiated by，and consequcntly generated upon, thc surface cut oul by this coneBut the earlh would intercept precisely as many meteols as would fall upon this small aiea, and would, thereforc, rcceive hcat from thc impact of a certain number of meteors upon itself,and as much heat from tho sun as would be generated by the impact of an equal number upon the sun

The heat derived by the earth from the two sources would be as the squares of the velocities with which the meteols strike the earth and sun. It was seen in Art 42 that this number is o. $2 - 6 0$ Therefore, if this meteoric hypothesis of the maintenance of tho sun's heat is correct, the earth should reccive rr as much heat from the impact of meteors as from the sun. This is cartninlv millions of umes moie heat than the carth receives from meteors, and consequently the thcoiy that the sun's heat is maintaned by the impact of meteois is not tenable.

45.Central Force． This chapler will bc devoled lo the discussion of the molion of a maleiial pailicle when subjecl to an altractivo ol rcpelling foice whoso lne of action always passcs though a fixed point. This fivcd point is called the cente) of force It is not mpled thal thc foico cmanales from the conlcr or thal there is bul onc forcc,but simply that tho rcsultanl of all the forces acting on tho pailclc always pagscs through this point The force may bc direclcd towaid tho point or from it,or part of the time towaid and part of the time fiom il. Il may be zero at any timc,but if tho pai ticle passcs through a point wherc lhe forco to which it is subjcat becomcs infinito, a spccial invcsligalion, which cannot be taken up herc,is rcquired to follow it farther.Since attractivo forccs aic of mosl ficqucnt occurrencc in astronomical and physical probloms, the formulas dovclopcd will bo for this case, a chango of sign of tho cocfficiont of imlenaty of lhc forco for unit distance will mako lhc formulas vald for the casc of rcpulaion

The origin of cobidinates will bc taken at thc center of force, and the linc"from thc oiigin to tho moving parliclo is called tho radius veclor. Tho path descrrbed by tho particlc is called lhe orbitThe orbits of this chapler aio plano curvegThe plancs are defincd by the position of the ccnter of foico and tho linc of initial projeclion. 46.The Law of Areas. Tho firsl problem will bo to dorive thc gencral properlics of motion which hold for all contral forceg.Tho first of thcso,which is of greal importance,is tho law of areas,and constitutes iho fisl Pioposilion of Nowlon's Prncima. Ii is, f a particle rs subyecl lo a central fm ce,the areas whrch are swept over by the radius veclor are proportional to the intervals of tme in whch they are described. The following is Newton's demonslration of it.

Let $o$ bo tho conler of foice,and lot the parliclo bo projeoted from $\pmb { A }$ in tho dicclion of $B$ wilh the vclocily AB. Thcn,by the frst law of motion, it would pass to $C ^ { \prime }$ m tho first two units of time if thele wele no external foices acting upon itBut suppose that when it arrives at B an instanianeous force acts upon it im the diection of the origin with such mtensity that it would movc to b in a unit of time if it had no previous motion.Then, by thc second law of motion,it will move along the diagonal of the parallelogram BbCC' to C. If no other force were applicd it would move with uniform velocity to D' in tho next unit of time. But suppose that when it arrives at C another instantancous force acts upon it in the direction of the oiigin with such intensity that it would move to c in a unit of time if it had no picvious motion Then,as before, it will move along the diagonal of the parallelogram and arrive at D at the end of the unit of timc. This piocess can be iepeated imdefinitely.

![](images/77d05f2949bc3616b4ed90f3885e39554183d9d176ffd965181f342a68f5fb4d.jpg)

The following equalities among the areas of the,trianglcs involved hold, since they have sequentially cqual bases and altitudes:

$$
O A B = O B C ^ { \prime } = O B C = O C D ^ { \prime } = O C D = \mathrm { { e t c } } ,
$$

Therefore，it follows that OAB = OBC = OCD = ODE,ctc. That is, the areas of the triangles swept over in the succccding unts of time are equal,and, therefoie, the sums of the areas of thc triangles descrbed in any intervals of time are propoitional to the intervals

The reasoning is true without any changes however small the mtervals of time may be Let the path be considcred for some fixed finite period of time Let the intervals into which ilis divided be taken shorter and shorter,the impulsos willbecome closer and closer togethei Suppose the ratio of the magnitudes of the impulscs to the values of the intervals between them remains finite, lhen tho broken line will become more and more nearly a smooth curvc Suppose the intervals of time approach zero as a lmit, the succesion of impulses will approach a contimuous force as almit,and the broken lme will approach a smooth cuive as a limit. The aleas swepl ovci by thc iadius vcctol m any finite mtei vals of time aie propoitonal lo lhesc mlcivals duiing the wholc hmiting process. Thciefote, the pioportionahly of areas holds al thc hmit,and thc thcoicm is true fol a continuous contial foice

It will be obscivcd that it is not necessaay that the cential force shall vary continuously. It may be altiactive and inslantaneously change lo repulsion,ol bccome zero,and thc law will still hold, but it is nccessaiy lo cxcludc thc caso wheie it becomcs infimte unless a spccial mvcstigation is made.

The lncar vclocity varies inveisely as the perpendiculau from the olgm upon the tangenl to the cui ve at the poinl of tho moving particle,foi, tho area describcd in a unit of time is cqual to the pioducl of the velocily and the pcrpendicular upon the tangent Since lhe aea desciibcd m a unil of timo is always the sane, it follows thal the lincar vclocily of tho pailiclo vaiies invcrsely as the pei pendicular fiom the origm lo lho tangent of its orbit

47. Analytical Demonstration of the Law of Areas. Although the language of Goomcliy was cmploycd in tho demonstralion of Ait 46,yet thc cssential clemenls of tho methods of thc Difforontial and Inlegral Calculus wcrc uscd Thus, in passing to tho hmit, thc pioccss was cssentially that of cxpressing tho problom m diffcicnlial cquations,and, in insisting upon comparing nleivals of fimito sizc when tho unils of mcasuicment were Indefinitcly decrcased, tho process of mtegration was ically employcd.Il will bc found that in the troatmenl of all problems mvolving variable forccs and molions thc methods aro in essenco thoso of tho Calculus,oven though the demonstiations be couched in geometrical language. It is porhaps casici to follow tho reasoning in gcometrical foim when ono cncountcrs it for the firsl timc; but tho processes aie allspceial and imvolve fundamental dimculties which are oflcn troublcsomo On the othcr hand, thc dovelopment of tho Caloulus is of the precisc form to adapt it to thc ticalmcnt of thcse problems,and afler ils piinciplcs have bccn once mastored,the apphcation of it 1s characteaized by comparativc simphcity and grcat gonoralty. A few problems will bo ticated by both mclhods to show their cssential samoness, and to illustratc the advantages of analysis,

Letf represent the acceleration to which the particle is subjecl. By hypothesis, tho lino of foice always passes through a fixed poit,which willbe taken as tho origin of coordimates.

$$

$$

On roferring to Art.16,it 1s seen that (2) may bo wriltera

$$

$$

where $\pmb { A }$ is the area swept over by the radius veclor. Tll of this equation is
