48.Converse of the Theorem of Areas. By hypothesis

$$
\pmb { \mathscr { s } } = \pmb { \mathscr { c } } _ { 1 } t + \pmb { \mathscr { c } } _ { 2 } .
$$

On takmg the dorivative with respeet to t,it is found that

$$
\frac { d \lambda } { d l } = c _ { 1 } .
$$

This equation becomes in polar coordinateg

$$
r ^ { 2 } { \frac { d \theta } { d t } } = 2 c _ { 1 } ,
$$

and in rectangular coordinateg

$$
x \frac { d y } { d t } - y \frac { d x } { d t } = 2 c _ { 1 } .
$$

Tho derivative of this oxpression with respect to t is

or

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { x \frac { d ^ { 2 } \mathcal { Y } } { d t ^ { 2 } } - \mathcal { Y } \frac { d ^ { 2 } \mathcal { X } } { d t ^ { 2 } } = 0 ; } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \mathcal { X } } { d t ^ { 2 } } : \frac { d ^ { 2 } \mathcal { Y } } { d t ^ { 2 } } = \infty : \mathcal { Y } . } } \end{array}
$$

That is, the compononts of accoleration are proporlional to the coordinatcs; thereforc,if tho law of areas is truo with respect to a point, the resultant of the accelerations passes through that point.

49. The Laws of Angular and Linear Velocity. From tho expression for the law of areas in polar coordinates, it follows that

$$
{ \frac { d \theta } { d t } } = { \frac { \hbar } { r ^ { 2 } } } ;
$$

therefore,the angular velocity is inversely proportional to the square   
of the radvus vector   
、The hnoar velocity is

$$
\frac { d \vartheta } { d t } = \frac { \widetilde { d } s } { d \vartheta } \frac { d \vartheta } { d t } = \frac { d \vartheta } { d \theta } \frac { \hbar } { \vartheta ^ { 2 } }
$$

Let p repicgent the perpendicular from the oiigin upon tho tangent; then it is known from Diffeiential Calculus that

$$

$$

Hence the expression for the lncal velocity becomes

$$
\frac { d s } { d t } = \frac { h } { p } ,
$$

therefore,the hnea velocrty zs inversely proportional to the perpendicular from the orgin upon the tangent.

SIMULTANEOUS DIFFERENTIAL EQUATIONS

50. The Order of a System of Simultaneous Differential Equations. One integral,equation (2),of tho diffeicntial cquations (1) has been found which the motion of the particle must fulfill The question is how many more integrals must be found in order to have the complete solution of the problem

The number of intcgrals which must be found to completcly solve ${ \mathfrak { a } }$ system of diffeiential equations is called the order of the systemThus, the equation

$$
\frac { d ^ { n } x } { d t ^ { n } } = c
$$

ls of the nth order, because it must be intcgrated $\pmb { n }$ times to bo reduced to an integral form Similarly, the general equation

$$
f _ { n } { \frac { d ^ { n } x } { d t ^ { n } } } + f _ { n - 1 } { \frac { d ^ { n - 1 } \tau } { d t ^ { n - 1 } } } + \cdot \cdot + f _ { 1 } { \frac { d x } { d t } } + f _ { 0 } = 0 ,
$$

where fn，: ,fo are functions of x and t, must be integrated n times in oider to expiess x as a function of t,and is of the nth order. 1

An equation of the nth order can be reduced to an equivalenl system of n simulianeous equatrons each of the first order. Thus,to reduco (6) to a simullaneous system,let

$$
\alpha _ { 1 } = \frac { d \alpha } { d t } , \qquad \alpha _ { 2 } = \frac { d \alpha _ { 1 } } { d t } , \qquad \cdot \quad \cdot \cdot , \quad \alpha _ { n - 1 } = \frac { d \alpha _ { n - 2 } } { d t } ;
$$

whence

$$
\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \begin{array} { l } { \frac { d v } { d t } = \mathfrak { x } _ { 1 } , } \\ { \frac { d v _ { 1 } } { d t } = \mathfrak { x } _ { 2 } , } \\ { \frac { d v _ { 2 } } { d t } = \mathfrak { x } _ { 3 } , } \end{array} } \\ { \begin{array} { l } { \frac { d v _ { 3 } } { d t } = \mathfrak { x } _ { 3 } , } \\ { \cdot \ \cdot \ \cdot \ \ \cdot \ \ \ \cdot \ \ \ \cdot } \\ { \frac { d v _ { n - 1 } } { d t } = - \frac { f _ { n - 1 } } { f _ { n } } \mathfrak { x } _ { n - 1 } - \ \cdot \ - \ \cdot \ \ \cdot \ \ \frac { f _ { 1 } } { f _ { n } } \mathfrak { x } _ { 1 } - \frac { f _ { 0 } } { f _ { n } } . } \end{array} } \end{array} \right.
$$

Thcrefore, these n simultancous cquations, cach of thc first oider, constilute & syslem of tho nth oideiAn cquation, ol & system of cquations,icduced to tho foim (7) is said io be icduced to tho noi mal form,and tho systcm is callcd a noi mal system.

Two simultancous equationg of oder m and n can bo reaucca to & normal system of oider m+ n. Consider the cquationg

$$
\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { f _ { m } \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { m } \mathfrak { c } } { d \ell ^ { m } } + \triangledown \cdot \cdot + f _ { \mathrm { \scriptscriptstyle t } } \displaystyle \frac { d \mathfrak { c } } { d \ell } + f _ { 0 } = 0 , } \\ { \phi _ { n } \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { n } \mathfrak { y } } { d \ell ^ { n } } + \dots + \phi _ { 1 } \displaystyle \frac { d \mathfrak { y } } { d \ell } + \phi _ { 0 } = 0 , } \end{array} \right.
$$

whero tho f,and the .arc functiong of x, V,and t By a substitution similar to that uscd m reducing (6),it follows that they are cquivalent to tho normal systom

$$
\left\{ \begin{array} { l }  \displaystyle \begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle \frac { d v } { d t } = x _ { 1 } , } \\ { \displaystyle \dot { \mathrm {  ~ \cdot ~ } } \mathrm {  ~ \cdot ~ } \mathrm {  ~ \cdot ~ } \mathrm {  ~ \cdot ~ } \mathrm {  ~ \cdot ~ } } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d v _ { n - 1 } } { d t } = - \frac { f _ { n - 1 } } { f _ { n } } x _ { n - 1 } - \mathrm {  ~ \cdot ~ } \mathrm {  ~ \cdot ~ } \mathrm {  ~ \cdot ~ } \frac { f _ { 1 } } { f _ { n } } x _ { 1 } - \frac { f _ { 0 } } { f _ { n } } , } \end{array} \right. \end{array}
$$

which is of tho ordor m+n. Evidently & simlar reduction is possible whon cach cquation contains derivatives with respect to both of tho variablcs, oither soparalely or as products.

Convorscly,a normal system of order n can in general be transformed rnto a srngle cqualion of mder n wilh onc dependent variable. To fix the ideas,consider the system of tho second ordcr

reduced after each integral is found. In mechanical and physical problems the intutions are impoitant in suggesting methods of treatment, so it is generally advantageous to uso such variables that thel geometrical and physical meanings shall bo casily perceivcd.For this reason,it 1s generally simpler nol to reduce the order of the problem after each integral is found.

$$

$$

55.Newton's Law of Gravitation. In the eaily parl of Ihm seventcenth contury Kepler announced threc laws of plunetnry motion,which he had dcrived fom a mosl laborious discuwutull of a long serics of obselvations of the plancts, cspeeially of Mara. They are the following.

LAW I The radius vector of each planet with respecl to lhe mum as the orrgin sweeps over equal areas in equal times.

LAW II. The obrt of each planet s an ellpse unth the sun al onp of rts focr

LAW III The squares of the perrods of the planets arc lo cach other as the cubes of the magor semz-axes of therr respcctivc obuts

It was on these laws that Newton based his demonstration thimt the plancts move subject to forces directed toward thc sun,und varying inversely as the squ&res of then distanccs from tho num. The Newtonian law will be derived here by cmploying tho anulytt" cal method mstead of the geometrical methods of tho Pinrimn.\*

From the converse of the theorem of areas and Keplor's fiat lnw, it follows that the planets move subjcdt to ccntral foiccs direlrl toward the sunThe cui ves described are given by tho rrroid law,and cquation (2b) can,theieforo,be uscd to find tho cxpreim for the accoleration in terms of the cooidimates. Let a ieprcrnt the majol semi-axis of the elipse,and e its eccentricity；then ila cquation in polar cooidinates with origin at $\mathfrak { a }$ focus is

$$
r = { \frac { a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } { 1 + e \cos \theta } } .
$$

Therefore

$$
u + \frac { \tiny { \breve { w } } ^ { 2 } u } { d \theta ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } .
$$

On substituting this expressonin (25),it is found that tho cqution for the acceleration 1s

$$
f = { \frac { \hbar ^ { 2 } } { a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } } \cdot { \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } } } = { \frac { l c ^ { 8 } } { r ^ { 2 } } } .
$$

\* Book $\bar { \mathbf { \delta } } _ { \mathbf { \nu } _ { 1 } }$ Proposition xi

Thcreforc, the acceleration to which any planel is subject vaiies inversely as tho square of its distancc froin the sum

If tho distance r is climinated by the polal cquation of thc conic the expression for/has tho form

$$
f = h _ { 1 } { } ^ { 2 } ( 1 + e \cos \theta ) ^ { 2 } ,
$$

which depends only upon tho dncction of the attracted body and not upon its distance.Now for points on the cllipso thc two expressions for f givc thc samo valuc, bul clsowheie they give diffeient values.It is cleai that many othcr laws of force,all agiceing in giving the sainc numeiical valucs of f for points on the ellpsc,can bc obtaincd by making other uses of the cquation of the conic to elminato r. For cxample,since il follows from the polar equation of tho elhpso for points on its circumfercnce that

$$
\frac { ( 1 + e \cos \theta ) r } { a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } = 1 ,
$$

one such law is

$$
f = \frac { \mathit { l } \mathfrak { c } ^ { 2 } ( 1 + \mathrm { ~ \mathfrak { c } ~ \cos ~ } \theta ) ^ { 3 } r } { \mathit { a } ( 1 - \mathrm { ~ \mathfrak { c } ^ { 2 } ~ } ) } ,
$$

and this valuo of f,which dopends both upon thc direction and distance of tho attracted body,diffeig from both of the preceding for pomts not on tho clipso. All of these laws are equally consistcnt with thc motion of tho planet in question as cxpresscd by Kopler's laws. Bul the laws of Koplor hold for cach of thc cight planets and the twenly-six known satollites of tho solar system, besides for moro than soven hundred small planels which have so far been discovered. It is natuial to imposc tho condition, if possiblo,that tho forco shall valy accordng to tho same law for each body. Smce thc occontiicitics and longitudcs of the perihclia of their orbits aro all difforent, the law of forco is lthe same for all these bodies only whon it has the form

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \int = \frac { \operatorname { \mathcal { k } } ^ { 2 } } { \hbar ^ { 2 } } . } \end{array}
$$

Another reason for adopting this cxpression for fis that in case of all tho others the altraction would dopend upon the direction of tho attracted body,and this scemg impiobable This cqnclusion is further supported by tho facl that tho forcos to which comets are subject when thoy movo through lhc cnlir syslem of plancts vary according to this law, And finally,as will be shown in Art. 89,tho accelerations to which the vaiious plancts are subjcct vary from one to another accoiding to this law.

From the consideration of Kepler's laws, the gravity at tho 3arth's surface,and thc motion of the moon aiound tho cai th, Newton was led to the enunciation of thc Law of Universal Gravitation,which is,every two particles of maie in the unwcrse attract each other wnth $^ { a }$ force which acts in the lne gorning them,and whose intensrty vastes as the poduct of their masses and rnversely as the squares of ther distance apart

It will be observed that thc law of gravitation involves considerably moie than can be derived fiom Keplcl's laws of planctary motion,and it was by a master stioko of genius thal Ncwlon grasped it in its immense gencrality,and slalcd it so cxactly thal it has stood without change for moic than 2oo yearsWhon contemplated in its entirety it is one of the grandest conceptiong in the physical sciences.

56. Examples of Finding the Law of Force. $( a )$ If a particle describes ${ \mathfrak { a } }$ circle passing through the oiigin，tho law of forco (depending on the distance alone) under which it moves 1s $\clubsuit$ very simple expression Let $\pmb { \alpha }$ represent the radius；then the polar equation of the circle is

$$
r = 2 a \cos \theta , ~ u = \frac { 1 } { 2 a \cos \theta } .
$$

Therefore

$$
{ \frac { d ^ { 2 } u } { d \theta ^ { 2 } } } + u = 8 a ^ { 2 } u ^ { 3 }
$$

On substituting this expression m (25),it is found that

$$
f = { \frac { 8 a ^ { 2 } \hbar ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 6 } } } = { \frac { \hbar ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 5 } } } .
$$

(b) Suppose the particle describes an cllipso with tho origin at the centcr. The polar cquation of an ellipso with tho center as origin 1s

$$
r ^ { 2 } = \frac { b ^ { 2 } } { 1 - e ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } .
$$

From this it follows that

$$
\left\{ \begin{array} { c } { { \dot { b } u = \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \displaystyle b \frac { d ^ { 2 } u } { d \theta ^ { 2 } } = \frac { e ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } \theta - e ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } } - \frac { e ^ { 4 } \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } { ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } \theta ) ^ { \sharp } } , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \displaystyle u + \frac { d ^ { 2 } u } { d \theta ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } { b ^ { 4 } } \cdot \frac { 1 } { u ^ { 3 } } . } } \end{array} \right.
$$

On substituting in (25),the expiession for fis found to be

$$
f = \frac { \bar { h } ^ { 2 } ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } { \bar { b } ^ { 1 } } \cdot r = \bar { k } ^ { 2 } r ,
$$

# TIn UNIVERSALITY OF NEWTON's LAW.

57.Double Star Orbits. The law of gravitation is proved from Keplel's laws and ccrtam assumptions as to its umquenoss to hold in thc solar syslem，the question whether it is actually unwersal naturally picsents itsclf The fixed stars aic so remoto thal il is not possble to obscivo planels iovolving alound them, if mdccd they havo such attendantsThc only observations thus far obtained which throw any lght upon tho subjccl aro those of the motions of the double starg.

Double slar astronomy staricd about 1780 with the search for close slais by Sir William IIcrschel for the puipose of determining parallax by tho differcntial mcthod A few years werc sufficiont to show him; to his grcat surprisc, that in somc cases the two components of a pair welo rovolving around cach other，and that, thcrefore, they woic physically connccted as well as being appargntly m the same part of the sky. The discovery and mcasurement of theso systems has bccn pursucd with increasing interest and zeal by astronomoisBurnham's great calaloguc of double stars conlains about 13,0oo of these objccts. The relalive motions are so slow in most cascs thal only a fow have ycl completcd ono rcvolulion, or enough of one rovolution so that tho shapcs of their orbits are known with corlainty. There aic now about thirty pairs whose observcd angular motions have bccn suficiently great to provo,wilhin tho eriors of the obscrvalions, that they move in elhpses with respcct to cach othcr in such a manncr that thc law of areas is fulfilled. In no case is the primary at the focus, or at the center,of tho relative cllipse doscribed by the companion, but it oocupics some othor place within the cllpso, tho position varving rroatly in difforent systcms.

From tho observations and tho convorso of tho law of areas it follows that thc resultant of the forces acting upon onc slar of a pair ig always dirocted toward the othcr. Thc law of variation of the intensity of tho forco doponds upon tho position in the elipse which the center of forco occupics. It must not be overlooked at this point that the orbits of tho stars are nol obsorved directly， but that it g thcir projeetions upon the planes tangent to the celestial sphere at their respectivo places which are scen The effect of this sorl of piojection is to change thc tiuo ellpse into a difterent apparent eilpse whose inajor axis has a difforent direction, and one that is differently situalcd wilh respccl to the ccntral stai;mdeed, it might happen thal if onc of tho stars was Ically n the foous of the tiue ellipse desciubed by the other, tho piojection would be such as to mako it le on the mmor axis of thc appalent ellipse.

、Astronomcrs have assumcd that the orbits are plano curves and that the appaient deparluic of the cential stai from lhe focus of the elhpse described by the companion is duo to projcction, and have then computed the angle of the line of nodes and tho inclination No inconsistencies aie introduccd in this way， but tho possbility remains that the assumptions are not true. The question of what the Iaw of force must be if it is not Newton's law of gravitation will now be investigated.

80. The Attraction of a Solid Homogeneous Ellipsoid upon an Exterior Particle. Ivory's Method，The integials become so complcated m tho caso of anexteror particle that the components of allraction havo not bcen found by direct integiation except in gorieg. They areo computed mdirectly by expressng them in torms of the components of attraotion of $\clubsuit$ related ellipsoid upon particles in its interiorThis artifice constitutes Ivory's method \*

![](images/63f84e5da0172945492a23410544f7227cfcd04b08f6e5a3a8083671b0abe20d.jpg)  
Fg 23.

Lot it bo roquired to find the attraction of the ellipsoid $\pmb { \mathcal { B } }$ upon the exterior particle $P ^ { \prime }$ at the point $( x ^ { \prime } , y ^ { \prime } , z ^ { \prime } )$ Let the semiaxes of $\pmb { \mathcal { B } }$ be $a , b ,$ and c. Construct through $P ^ { \prime } ~ { \mathfrak { a n } }$ ellipsoid $\mathcal { B ^ { \prime } } _ { \pmb { \mathscr { s } } }$ confocal with ${ \mathbf { } } { \mathbf { } } { \mathbf { } }$ with the sem1-axes $a ^ { \prime } , b ^ { \prime } , c ^ { \prime } .$ and suppose it hag the same donsity as $\pmb { \mathcal { B } }$ .The axes of the lwo ellipsoids are related by the equations

$$
\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { a ^ { \prime } = \sqrt { a ^ { 2 } + \kappa } , } \\ { b ^ { \prime } = \sqrt { b ^ { 2 } + \kappa } , } \\ { c ^ { \prime } = \sqrt { c ^ { 2 } + \kappa } , } \end{array} \right.
$$

where $\pmb { \kappa }$ is defined by the equation

$$
\frac { x ^ { \prime 2 } } { a ^ { 2 } + \kappa } + \frac { y ^ { \prime 2 } } { \tilde { b } ^ { 2 } + \kappa } + \frac { z ^ { \prime 2 } } { c ^ { 2 } + \kappa } - 1 = 0
$$

The only value of $\pmb { \kappa }$ admissible m this problem 1s real and positive, Equation (46) 18 $\mathfrak { a }$ cubic in k and has one positive and two negative roots,for,the left member considered as a function of $\pmb { \kappa }$ 1s negat1vo when $\kappa = + \infty$ ，positive，when $\kappa = 0$ (because $( x ^ { \prime } , ~ y ^ { \prime } , ~ z ^ { \prime } )$ is exterior to theellipsoid $\pmb { \mathcal { B } }$ ；positive,when $\kappa = - \ c ^ { 2 } + \epsilon$ (where $\pmb { \epsilon }$ is a very small positive quantity)；negative, when $\kappa = - \ c ^ { 2 } - \epsilon ;$ positive, when $\kappa = - \ b ^ { 3 } + \epsilon$ ，negative,when $\kappa = - \bar { b } ^ { 2 } - \epsilon ;$ positave,when $\kappa = - a ^ { 2 } + \epsilon$ negative,when $\kappa = - \ a ^ { 2 } - \epsilon _ { i }$ and negative when $\kappa = - \infty$ .The graph of the function is given in Fig. 24. When the positive root is taken, $a ^ { \prime } , \ b ^ { \prime }$ ，and ${ \pmb { c } } ^ { \prime }$ are determined uniquely

# THE PROBLEM OF TWO BODIES

83.Equations of Motion It will be assumed in this chapter that the two bodies are spheres and homogeneous m concenliic layers Then,in accoidance wilh tho iesults obtaincd m Arl.69, they will attiact each other with a foice which is proportional lo the product of their masses and which vaiies mvcisely as the square of the distance betwecn their centeis

Let mi and mg repiesent the masscs of the two bodies, and m+ m= M Choose an arbitraiy system of 1ectangulai axcs in space and let the cooidinates of mi and mz refericd to it bo respectively (51, n, 5i) and (\$2, nz,52)Lel the dislance belwcen m1 and mz be denoted byr; then it follows from thc laws of motion and the law of gravitation that the differential equations whnch the cobrdnates of the bodics satisfy are

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { [ m _ { 1 } \frac { \partial \xi _ { 1 } } { \partial t ^ { 3 } } = - k ^ { 3 } m _ { 1 } m _ { 1 } \frac { ( \xi _ { 1 } - \xi _ { 1 } ) } { r ^ { 3 } } ,  } \\ & {  [ m _ { 1 } \frac { \partial \xi _ { 1 1 } } { \partial t ^ { 5 } } = - k ^ { 3 } m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } \frac { ( \xi _ { 1 } - \eta _ { 1 } ) } { r ^ { 3 } } ,   } \\ & {   [ m _ { 1 } \frac { \partial \xi _ { 1 } } { \partial t ^ { 5 } } = - k ^ { 2 } m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } \frac { ( \xi _ { 1 } - \xi _ { 2 } ) } { r ^ { 3 } } ,    } \\ & {        [ m _ { 2 } \frac { \partial \xi _ { 3 } } { \partial t ^ { 5 } } = - k ^ { 2 } m _ { 2 } m _ { 1 } \frac { ( \xi _ { 3 } - \xi _ { 1 } ) } { r ^ { 2 } } ,      } \\ & {     m _ { 2 } \frac { \partial \xi _ { 3 } } { \partial t ^ { 5 } } = - k ^ { 2 } m _ { 3 } m _ { 1 } \frac { ( \xi _ { 3 } - \eta _ { 1 } ) } { r ^ { 3 } } ,     } \\ & {    m _ { 3 } \frac { \partial \eta _ { 1 } } { \partial t ^ { 5 } } = - k ^ { 2 } m _ { 3 } m _ { 1 } \frac { ( \xi _ { 1 } - \eta _ { 1 } ) } { r ^ { 3 } } ,    } \\ & {     m _ { 3 } \frac { \partial \xi _ { 3 } } { \partial t ^ { 5 } } = - k ^ { 2 } m _ { 2 } m _ { 1 } \frac { ( \xi _ { 3 } - \xi _ { 1 } ) } { r ^ { 3 } } .   } \end{array}
$$

In order to solve these six simultaneous equations of the second order twelve integrals must be found. They willintroduce twelvo arbitrary constants of integration which can be dclermincd in any particular case by the three initial coordinates and the lhrco components of the initial velocity of cach of tho boiles

84.The Motion of the Center of Mass. On adding thc first and fourth, thc sccond and fifth,and thc thnd and sxth equations of thc system (1),il is found that

$$
\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { m _ { 1 } \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } \xi _ { 1 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } + m _ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } \xi _ { 2 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } = 0 , } \\ { m _ { 1 } \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } \eta _ { 1 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } + m _ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } \eta _ { 2 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } = 0 , } \\ { m _ { 1 } \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } \xi _ { 1 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } + m _ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } \xi _ { 2 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } = 0 } \end{array} \right.
$$

These equations are immediately integiablc, and give

$$
\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { m _ { 1 } \displaystyle \frac { d \xi _ { 1 } } { d t } + m _ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { d \xi _ { 2 } } { d t } = \alpha _ { 1 } , } \\ { m _ { 1 } \displaystyle \frac { d \eta _ { 1 } } { d t } + m _ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { d \eta _ { 2 } } { d t } = \beta _ { 1 } , } \\ { m _ { 1 } \displaystyle \frac { d \xi _ { 1 } } { d t } + m _ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { d \xi _ { 2 } } { d t } = \gamma _ { 1 } . } \end{array} \right.
$$

On integrating again thcy bccomo

$$
\left\{ { \begin{array} { l } { m _ { 1 } \xi _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } \xi _ { 2 } = \alpha _ { 1 } \ell + \alpha _ { 2 } , } \\ { m _ { 1 } \eta _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } \eta _ { 2 } = \beta _ { 1 } \ell + \beta _ { 2 } , } \\ { m _ { 1 } \xi _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } \xi _ { 2 } = \gamma _ { 1 } \ell + \gamma _ { 2 } . } \end{array} } \right.
$$

Thus,six of tho twclve inlegrals are found,the abitiary conslants of integiation bcing $\alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \beta _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 2 } , \gamma _ { 1 } , \gamma _ { 2 }$

Let $\overline { { \xi } } , \ \overline { { \eta } } ,$ and $\overline { { \xi } }$ be thc coordinates of the conter of mass of tho system； then il follows fiom Ail.19 and cquations (3) that

$$
\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { M _ { \xi } ^ { \prime } = m _ { 1 } \xi _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } \xi _ { 2 } = \alpha _ { 1 } \ell + \alpha _ { 2 } , } \\ { M _ { \eta } ^ { \prime } = m _ { 1 } \eta _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } \eta _ { 2 } = \beta _ { 1 } \ell + \beta _ { 2 } , } \\ { M _ { \xi } ^ { \prime } = m _ { 1 } \xi _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } \xi _ { 2 } = \gamma _ { 1 } \ell + \gamma _ { 2 } . } \end{array} \right.
$$

From thesc equalions it follows that tho cooidinates increase directly as the timc,and, thei cforo,that the center of mass moves with uniform vclocily ${ \bf 0 r } ,$ laking their derivatives, squaring, and adding,it is found that

$$
M ^ { 2 } \left\{ \left( \frac { d \overline { { \xi } } } { d \ell } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { d \overline { { \eta } } } { d \ell } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { d \overline { { \xi } } } { d \ell } \right) ^ { 2 } \right\} = c _ { 1 } { } ^ { 2 } + \beta _ { 1 } { } ^ { 2 } + \gamma _ { 1 } { } ^ { 2 } ;
$$

whence

$$
\overline { { { \widetilde { V } } } } = \frac { \sqrt { \alpha { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \beta { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \gamma { 1 } ^ { 2 } } } { M } ,
$$

where V represents the speed with which the center of mags moves. The speed is therefore constant

On elminating t fiom (4),it is found that

$$
\frac { \bar { M } \overline { { \xi } } - \alpha _ { 2 } } { \alpha _ { 1 } } = \frac { \bar { M } \overline { { \eta } } - \beta _ { 2 } } { \beta _ { 1 } } = \frac { \bar { M } \overline { { \xi } } - \gamma _ { 2 } } { \gamma _ { 1 } } .
$$

The cobrdinates of the cenler of mass fulfill these ielations which aie the symmetrical equations of a stiaight line in space, therefole, the center of mass moves in a strarght lne wnth constant speed.

85.The Equations for Relative Motion. Take a new system of axes paralel to the old,but with the origin at the center of mass of the two bodies Let the co8rdinates of mi and mz referred to thig ncw system be x1, Vi, z1 and xz, y2, &2 respectively. They are related to the old coordinates by the equations

$$
\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { x _ { 1 } = \xi _ { 1 } - { \vec { \xi } } , } & { x _ { 2 } = \xi _ { 2 } - { \vec { \xi } } , } \\ { y _ { 1 } = \eta _ { 1 } - { \vec { \eta } } , } & { y _ { 2 } = \eta _ { 2 } - { \vec { \eta } } , } \\ { z _ { 1 } = \xi _ { 1 } - { \vec { \xi } } ; } & { z _ { 2 } = \xi _ { 2 } - { \vec { \xi } } . } \end{array} \right.
$$

On substituting in (1), the differential equations of motion in the new variables are found to be

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \left[ \begin{array} { l } { m _ { 1 } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } g _ { 1 1 } } { \partial t ^ { 3 } } = - \mathcal { B } m _ { 1 } m _ { 1 } \frac { ( \mathcal { B } _ { 1 } - \mathcal { B } _ { 2 } ) } { r ^ { 3 } } , } \\ { m _ { 1 } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } g _ { 1 1 } } { \partial t ^ { 5 } } = - \mathcal { B } m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } \frac { ( g _ { 1 } - \mathcal { B } _ { 2 } ) } { r ^ { 3 } } , } \\ { m _ { 1 } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } g _ { 1 2 } } { \partial t ^ { 5 } } = - \mathcal { B } m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } \frac { ( g _ { 1 } - \mathcal { B } _ { 2 } ) } { r ^ { 3 } } , } \\ { m _ { 1 } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } g _ { 1 1 } } { \partial t ^ { 5 } } = - \mathcal { B } m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } \frac { ( g _ { 1 } - \mathcal { B } _ { 2 } ) } { r ^ { 3 } } , } \\ { m _ { 1 } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } g _ { 1 2 } } { \partial t ^ { 5 } } = - \mathcal { B } m _ { 2 } m _ { 1 } \frac { ( g _ { 1 } - \mathcal { B } _ { 2 } ) } { r ^ { 3 } } , } \\ { m _ { 2 } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } g _ { 1 3 } } { \partial t ^ { 5 } } = - \mathcal { B } m _ { 3 } m _ { 1 } \frac { ( g _ { 1 } - \mathcal { B } _ { 1 } ) } { r ^ { 3 } } , } \\ { m _ { 2 } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } g _ { 2 1 } } { \partial t ^ { 5 } } = - \mathcal { B } m _ { 2 } m _ { 1 } \frac { ( g _ { 2 } - \mathcal { B } _ { 2 } ) } { r ^ { 3 } } , } \end{array} \right] , } \end{array}
$$

which are of the samc form as the equations for absolute motion.

If the two systems m1, mg,and mz,mg are considered, and tho ratio of their periods is taken,it is found that

$$

$$

Jf the two systoms are composed of the sun and two planets respectively, then M1,z and Ms,2 arc very nearly equal becauso thc masses of the planets are excecdingly small compared to that of the sun. Thorefore, this equation becomes very nearly

$$

$$

or,the squares of the periodic times of the planets are proportional to the cubes of therr mean dastances, This is Kepler's third law.

It is to be observed that,in taking lhe ratios of the peiiods, it was assumed that I has the same value for the different planots, that is, that the sun's acceleration of the two planets would bo the same at unit distance On the other hand,it follows from the last equation,which Kepler establshed directly by observations, that k has the same value for the various planets. 95.Solution of Kepler's Equation. It will be shown first that Kepler's cquation always has one,and only one,real solution for every value of M and for every e such that O之e<1. Write the equation in the form

$$
\phi ( \vec { z } ) \equiv E - e \sin \vec { z } - M = 0
$$

Suppose $\pmb { \mathcal { M } }$ has somc given value between nπ and $( n + 1 ) \pi ,$ where $^ { n }$ is any mteger; then there is but one real value of $\pmb { \mathcal { D } }$ satisfying this equation, and it les betwecn nπ and $( n + 1 ) \pi$ $\mathfrak { P o r } _ { \pmb { \imath } }$ the function $\phi (  { \mathbb { E } } )$ when ${ \pmb { \mathscr { B } } } = { \pmb { n } } { \pmb { \pi } }$ is

$$
\phi ( n \pi ) = n \pi - M < 0 .
$$

And $\phi ( \overrightarrow { \mathbfit { B } } )$ when $\pmb { \mathcal { B } } = ( n + 1 ) \pi$ 18

$$
\phi [ ( n + 1 ) \pi ] = ( n + 1 ) \pi - M > 0
$$

Consequently there is an odd number of real solutions for E which le bctween nπ and (n +1)π But the deiivativo

$$
\phi ^ { \prime } ( \mathcal { B } ) \equiv 1 - \mathcal { e } \cos \mathcal { B }
$$

1s always positive； therefoic (E） incicascs continually with E and takes thc value zelo but once.

A convenicnt method of piaclically solving the equation is by means of an expansion due to Lagrange Suppose & is dcfined as $\mathbf { a }$ function of $\pmb { w }$ by tho cquation

$$
{ z } = { w } + \alpha \phi ( z ) ,
$$

wherc α is a paiamcteiLagrange has shown that any function of z can bc expresscd m a power series in α, which converges for suffcicntly small valucs of &, of the form\*

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { I ^ { \prime } ( z ) = F ( w ) + \frac { \alpha } { 1 } \cdot \phi ( w ) F ^ { \prime } ( w ) + \frac { \alpha ^ { 2 } } { 1 \cdot 2 } \frac { \partial } { \partial w } [ \{ \phi ( w ) \} ^ { 2 } I ^ { \prime } ( w ) ] } \\ { \qquad + \cdot \cdot + \frac { \alpha ^ { n + 1 } } { ( n + 1 ) ! } \frac { \partial ^ { n } } { \partial w ^ { n } } [ \{ \phi ( w ) \} ^ { n + 1 } F ^ { \prime } ( w ) ] + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } \end{array} \right. } \end{array}
$$

This expansion can bo appled to the solution of Kepler's cquation by writing it

$$
E = M + e \sin { B } ,
$$

96.Differential Corrections. A method will now be explained in one of its simplest applications, which 1s of great importance in many astronomical pioblemsSuppose an approximate value ofE is determined by the frst three terms of (45).Call it Eo; it is required to find the coriect value of E

$$

$$

$$

102.Position in Hyperbolic Orbits. There are close analogies between this problem and that of finding the position of a body im an clliptic orbit.But it follows fiom the polar equation of the hyperbola,

$$
r = \frac { a ( \epsilon ^ { 2 } - 1 ) } { 1 + \epsilon \cos \upsilon } ,
$$

where a is its major somi-axis and e its cccentuicity, that in this ${ \mathfrak { c a s e } } \ { \mathfrak { v } } { \mathrm { ~ c a n ~ v a r y ~ o n l y ~ f i o m - } } \pi + { \mathfrak { c o s } } ^ { - 1 } \left( { \frac { 1 } { \epsilon } } \right) { \mathfrak { t o } } + \pi - { \mathfrak { c o s } } ^ { - 1 } \left( { \frac { 1 } { \epsilon } } \right)$

The integrals of areas and vis viva are respeclively im the case of hypeibolic orbits

$$
\left\{ \begin{array} { r } { r ^ { 2 } \frac { d v } { d t } = k \sqrt { ( 1 + m ) a ( \epsilon ^ { 2 } - 1 ) } , } \\ { \left( \frac { d r } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } + r ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { d v } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } = k ^ { 2 } ( 1 + m ) \left( \frac { 2 } { r } + \frac { 1 } { a } \right) . } \end{array} \right.
$$

On elminating v from thc second of these cquations by meang of the first and solving,it is found that

$$
a \nu d \ell = { \frac { r \dot { d r } } { \sqrt { ( a + r ) ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } \epsilon ^ { 2 } } } } ,
$$

where

$$
\nu = { \frac { \kappa \sinh + m } { a ^ { 1 } } } .
$$

This equation can be integrated at oncc in terms of hypeibolic functions, but it is pieferable to introduce firsl an auxihary quantity F corresponding to the eccentric anomaly in olliptic orbits Let

$$
a + r = \frac { a \epsilon } { 2 } \left( e ^ { k } + e ^ { - E r } \right) = a \epsilon \cosh \ R ^ { \prime } ;
$$

then

$$
p d t = \Big \{ \frac { \ d } { \ d t } \frac { 1 } { \ d t } + \frac { \epsilon } { 2 } \left( e ^ { F } + e ^ { - F } \right) \Big \} d \bar { l } ^ { t } = [ - 1 + \epsilon \cosh \bar { l } ^ { t } ] d \bar { l } ^ { t } .

104.The Helocentric Position in the Ecliptic System. Mcthods have been given for finding the positions im the oibits in the vailous cases which aiiseThe formulas will now be derived fol determiming the posilion refeied to diffoienl systems of axes. Positions of bodics m the solai syslem are usually 1efeired to one of two systcms of coordinatcs, the ccliptic syslcm，o thc equatoral system. The tundamenlal planc in the cclptic systcm Is the plane of the caith's oibit,in the equatoial system il is tho plane of the eaith's equatorThc zcro poinl of thc fundamenial cucles in both systems is the vei nal cqunor,ol thc point at which the ccliptic cuts the cquatol fiom south to noilh,and is donotcd by The polar cooidinates in the ccliplic systein aie called longtude and lattude;and in the equatorial,rzght ascension and declinationWhcn the oiigm is at the sun Roman lcticis aro used to 1epiesent the coordinates,and when at tho eailh,Greck. Thus

In piactice α and d are very seldom used． Absolute positions of fundamental stars are given in the equalorial systcm, and tho obscrvcd positions of comels aie detormined by comparison with them， In somc theories relating to plancts and comcts,cspceially in considering the mulual perturbation of planets and their perturbations of comcts,it is moie convenieni to use thc ccliptic system, hence it is neccssary to be able to transform the cquations fromone system to the other.

The ascending node is tho piojeclion on tho ccliptic, from tho sun,of the place at which the body crosscs the plane of the ecliplio from south to north. It is measured from a fixcd poinl in thc ceeliptic, the vernal equinox,and is denoted by &． The projeclion of the point where the body crosscs the plane of the ccliptic from north to south 1s called tho descending node, and is donotcd by .

The inchnation is the angle between the plane of the orbit and the plane of thc ecliptic,and is denoted by 2. It has been the custom of some wiiteis to take the inclnation always less than 90°,and to defino the direction of motion as du ect ol etogi ade, according as it is the same as that of the earth or the opposite Anothel method that has been used is to considei all motion direct and the inclination as vaiying from O° to 180°The latter method avoids the use of double signs in the foimulas and is adopted here [Sce Art 86] Tho node and mclination define the position of the plane of the oibit in space

The distance fiom the ascending node to the peuihelion point counted in the direction of the motion of the body m its orbit is w, and defines the oricnlation of the oibit in its planeThe longitude of the perhelon is denoted by $\pi ,$ and is given by the equation

$$
\pi = \beta + \omega
$$

This element is nol a longitude in the oidinary sense because it Is counted in lwo diffeient plancg

The problcm of clalivo motion of two bodies was of the sivth order (Ait.85),and m thc mtegialion six abtray constants were introduccd Thoio aie six eloments, therefore, which are independent funclions of thcsc constants.They ale

${ a = \mathrm { m a j o r } }$ semi-axis,wlich defines the size of the orbit and the period of revolution.   
${ \pmb \mathscr { e } } =$ lhe cccentiicity,which defines the shape of the orbit   
$\begin{array} { r } { \Omega = } \end{array}$ longitude of ascendmg node, and   
${ \dot { \pmb { \imath } } } =$ mclnalion lo plane of the ccliptic,which together define the position of the plane of the oibit   
$\omega =$ Iongilude of the periholion point measured fiom the node, or $\pi =$ longiludo of the peihelion,either definmg the orienlation of the orbit m its plane   
$\pmb { T } =$ lime of perihelon passage, defining, with the other elements,the position of the body in its oibit at any time.

The polar cobidimates have been computed, hence the rectangular coordinales with tho positive end of the $\pmb { v }$ -axis directed to the peuhelion point and the $\pmb { \mathscr { Y } }$ -axis in the plane of the orbit are given by the cquations

$$

$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { x = r \cos { ( \upsilon + \omega ) } = r \cos { ( \upsilon + \pi - \delta ) } , } \\ { y = r \sin { ( \upsilon + \omega ) } = r \sin { ( \upsilon + \pi - \delta ) } , } \\ { z = 0 } \end{array} \right. } \end{array}
$$

The longitude of the body in its orbit counted from the ascendint node is called the argument of the latatude and is denotcd by u It is given by tho equation

$$
u = v + \omega ;
$$

hence u is known when v has been found,

105.Transfer of the Origin to the Earth. Let 写,H, Z be the geocentric cobrdinates of thc center of the sun referred to a system of nxcs with the $\pmb { x }$ -axis dirccted to the vernal equinox, and the $\pmb { \mathcal { Y } }$ -axis m the planc of the ccliplc. Let $\mathbf { P } , \boldsymbol { \Lambda } ,$ and $\mathbf { B } ^ { * }$ represent the gcocentric distancc,longiiude,and latitude of the sun respectively These quantitics aro givcn in the Nautacal Almanac for every day in tho yoar. The icctangular co8rdinates are expressed in terms of thom by

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \Xi ^ { \prime } = \mathrm { \bf ~ P } \cos \mathrm { \bf ~ B } \cos \mathrm { \bf ~ A } , } \\ { \mathrm { \bf ~ I I } = \mathrm { \bf ~ P } \cos \mathrm { \bf ~ B } \sin \mathrm { \bf ~ A } , } \\ { \mathrm { \bf ~ Z } = \mathrm { \bf ~ P } \sin \mathrm { \bf ~ B } . } \end{array} \right. } \end{array}
$$

The anglo B is gencrally less than a sccond of arc,and unless great accuraoy is rcquired theso oqualions may be replaced by

$$
\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \Xi ^ { \sharp } = \mathrm { \bf ~ P } \cos \Lambda , } \\ { \mathrm { \ I I } = \mathrm { \bf ~ P } \sin \Lambda , } \\ { \mathrm { \bf ~ Z } = 0 . } \end{array} \right.
$$

Let t",n",and 5" be the geocentric, and a",v",and z the heliocontric, cobidinatcs of the body with the x-axis directed toward tho veinal cqunox and tho y-axis in the plane of the ccliptin.Therefore

$$
{ } ^ { * } \operatorname { P } , \ \Lambda , \ \mathrm { B } \ \approx \operatorname { c a p i t a l } \rho , \lambda , \beta
$$

$$
\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \boldsymbol { \xi } ^ { \prime \prime } = \boldsymbol { x } ^ { \prime \prime } + \boldsymbol { \Xi } , } \\ { \boldsymbol { \eta } ^ { \prime \prime } = \boldsymbol { y } ^ { \prime \prime } + \mathrm { I I } , } \\ { \boldsymbol { \xi } ^ { \prime \prime } = \boldsymbol { z } ^ { \prime \prime } + \mathrm { I } } \end{array} \right.
$$

In polar cobidinates theso cquations are

$$
\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \rho \cos \beta \cos \lambda = r \cos \ b \cos \ l + \mathrm { P } \cos \mathrm { B } \ \cos \Lambda , } \\ { \rho \cos \beta \sin \lambda = r \cos \ b \sin \ l + \mathrm { P } \cos \mathrm { B } \sin \Lambda , } \\ { \rho \sin \beta \ \qquad = r \sin \upsilon \qquad + \mathrm { P } \sin \mathrm { B } } \end{array} \right.
$$

From these cquations $\lambda$ and $\beta$ can be found,but this syte y be taansformed into one which is more convenicnt by multiplymg the fisl cqualion by cos $\pmb { \Lambda }$ ,the second by sin $\pmb { \Lambda }$ ，and adding thc ploducts，and then multiplying thc first by -sin A and tho second by cos A,and adding the ploductgThe results aic

$$

$$

# CHAPTER VI

# THE DETERMINATION OF ORBITS

108 General Consideration. In discussing the pioblem of two bodies [Ails. 86-88] it was shown how the conslants of mtegration which aiise when the difteiential cquations are solved can be determincd m terms of the ouginal values of the cooidinales and of thc componcnls of velocity,and then il was shown how the clements of the conic section orbit can be deteimincd m tcims of thesc conslantsConsequenlly,it is nalural'to scck to dctormine the posilion and components of the observed body at some epochThe difcully ariscs from tho fact thal tho observations, wleh arc made from the imoving caith, givc only the diection of the objecl as seen by the obsorver,and fuinish no duoct mfoimation respecting ils dslancoAn observation of apparent position simply delormines the facl thal the body is somowhcre on onc half of a defincd lmo passing thiough tho observei．Thc position of the body m space is thcreforo nol given,and,of couise,its components of velocity are not dctermined． It becomcs neccssary on this accounl to sccuio additional obscrvaliong al olhcr times In tho intoival of time befoic tho sccond observalion is mado tho carth will have movcd and tho obsorved body will havo gone to another placo in ils orbit The sccond obsei vation simply determines anolhcr lnc on which the body is located at another dato. It is clcar that tho problom of finding tho position of the body and the clcmonls of its orbil from such data presonts some difculties

The first question lo selle 1s naturally tho number of observations whieh are neccssary m ordor that il shall bc possiblo to doterinine thc clemonts of the oibit. Since an orbil is defined by six olements, il follows that six indepondonl quantitics must be givcn by the observaliong in order that tho clomcnts may be deteimincd. A smglo complcto observation gives two quantitics, the angular courdinates of the body. Theroforo thrce complete observalions aro just sufficient, so far as thcse consideations aie concerned, to define its orbit Il is at lcast certain that no smaller number will suffice If the observed body is a comet whose orbit is a parabola, the cccentricity is unity and only fivo elements are to be found. In this case two complete obsoi vations and ono obsel vation giving one of the two angulai coordmates aie cnough.

109.Intermediate Elements. The apparent positons of tho observed body are usually obtaincd by mcasuiing its angular distances and directions from neighboiing fixed stais Since the stais are catalogued in Iight ascension and declination thc results come out m these coordinatcs,but thcy can,of course,be changed to the cchptac system, ol any olher,if il is desicd.

Supposo tho observations aic made at the times l, tz, and la, and let the coriesponding coordinates be dcnoled by their usual symbols having the subscripts 1,2,and 3 1cspectively. Tho right ascensions and dcelinations are functions of the elements of the O1bit and the datcs of obsorvation. Thcse relations may bo represenied by

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \alpha _ { 1 } = \varphi ( \Omega , ~ \psi _ { 1 } ~ \omega _ { 0 } , ~ a _ { 1 } ~ \varrho , ~ \eta _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } ; ~ t _ { 1 } ) , } \\ { \alpha _ { 2 } = \varphi ( \Omega , ~ \psi _ { 2 } ~ \omega _ { 0 } , ~ a _ { 2 } ~ \varrho , ~ \eta _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } ; ~ t _ { 2 } ) , } \\ { \alpha _ { 8 } = \varphi ( \Omega , ~ \psi _ { 3 } ~ \omega _ { 0 } , ~ a _ { 3 } ~ \varrho , ~ \eta _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } ; ~ t _ { 8 } ) , } \\ { \delta _ { 1 } = \psi ( \Omega , ~ \upsilon _ { 2 } ~ \omega _ { 0 } , ~ a _ { 1 } ~ \varrho , ~ \eta _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } ; ~ t _ { 1 } ) , } \\ { \delta _ { 2 } = \psi ( \Omega , ~ \upsilon _ { 3 } ~ \omega _ { 0 } , ~ a _ { 2 } ~ \varrho , ~ \eta _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } ; ~ t _ { 2 } ) , } \\ { \delta _ { 3 } = \psi ( \Omega , ~ \upsilon _ { 3 } ~ \omega _ { 0 } , ~ a _ { 3 } ~ \varrho , ~ \eta _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } ; ~ t _ { 3 } ) . } \end{array} \right. } \end{array}
$$

The problem consists in solving theso siv cquations for the six unknown clements. Thc funotions 𝜑 and  aro highly transcondental and imvolvo the olomonts in a very complicatcd fashion. In the caso of an cllipse tho pogition m the orbit is found by passing through Kopler's cquation,in thc hyperbola tho proccss is similar, and in the parabola a cubic cquation musl be solved； and in all three cases tho coordinalcs with respccl to the carth aro obtained by a number of trigonomotrical transformations. IIencc it 1s clear lhat there is no dircct solution of cquations (1） by odinary processes.

Although the ultimatc objecl is lo dolermine the clements of the orbit,tho problom of fnding other quantities which defino the elomonts may bo treatcd first. Thoso quantitics may bo congidered as being intermediate clements. It has bcon romarked that if the coordinates and the compononts of volocity are known at any cpoch, tho clemonts can bo found. Supposo it is desircd to find tho polar coordinates and thcir derivatives,which dotermine uniquely thc rectangular cooidinates and then derivatives, at the lime of tho second observation tz The equations corre Bponding to (1) becomo for this problem

$$
\begin{array}{c} \begin{array}{c} \{ \begin{array} { l l l l l l l l l l l l l l l l l } { \alpha _ { 1 } = \int ( \alpha _ { 2 } , } & { \delta _ { 2 } , } & { \rho _ { 2 } , } & { \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , } & { \delta _ { 2 } , } & { \rho _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , } & { i _ { 1 } , i _ { 2 } } \end{array} ) ,  \\ { \alpha _ { 2 } = \alpha _ { 2 } , } \\ { \alpha _ { 3 } = \int ( \alpha _ { 2 } , } & & & & & & \\ { \alpha _ { 3 } = \int ( \alpha _ { 2 } , } & { \delta _ { 2 } , } & { \rho _ { 2 } , } & { \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , } & { \delta _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , } & { \rho _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , } & { i _ { 3 } , i _ { 3 } } \end{array} ) ,  \\ { \delta _ { 1 } = g ( \alpha _ { 2 } , } & { \delta _ { 2 } , } & { \rho _ { 2 } , } & { \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , } & { \delta _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , } & { \rho _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , } & { i _ { 1 } , i _ { 2 } ) , } \\ { \delta _ { 2 } = \delta _ { 2 } , } & & & & & \\ { \delta _ { 3 } = g ( \alpha _ { 2 } , } & { \delta _ { 2 } , } & { \rho _ { 2 } , } & { \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , } & { \delta _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , } & { \rho _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , } & { i _ { 2 } , i _ { 3 } ) , } \end{array} 
$$

where

$$
{ \alpha _ { 2 } } ^ { \prime } = \frac { d \alpha } { d t } , \qquad \delta _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = \frac { d \delta } { d t } , \qquad { \rho _ { 2 } } ^ { \prime } = \frac { d \rho } { d t } \quad \mathrm { a t } \quad t = t _ { 2 } ,
$$

Snce ag and δz aio observed quantaties only the first, third,fourth, and sixth cquations ae to be solved for the four unknowns pz, αz', ${ \delta _ { \mathfrak { A } } } ^ { \prime }$ and $\rho \mathbf { 2 } ^ { \prime }$ .The problem is thoreforo 1educed to the solution of four simullaneous cquations, and they aro moreover much simpler than (1). Thcso cquations can bo put in a manageable form,and thig is,in facl,oncof th mcthods of treating the problemIt was first developed and applied to the actual dotermmation of orbits by Laplace in 1780,and it has been somewhat extended and modified as to dotails by many lator writers.

Asanother sot of intormediale clomonts tho thrce coordinates at two cpochs may bo taken, Suppose the times $\mathbf { \boldsymbol { \mathbf { \mathit { t } _ { 1 } } } }$ and $\pmb { \ell _ { 3 } }$ are chosen for thie purpose.Then tho fundamcntal cquations corresponding to (1)can bo written in tho form

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \alpha _ { 1 } = \alpha _ { 1 } , } \\ { \alpha _ { 2 } = \widetilde { I } ^ { * } ( \alpha _ { 1 } , ~ \delta _ { 1 } , ~ \rho _ { 1 } , ~ \alpha _ { 3 } , ~ \delta _ { 3 } , ~ \rho _ { 3 } ; ~ t _ { 1 } , ~ t _ { 2 } , ~ t _ { 3 } ) , } \\ { \alpha _ { 3 } = \alpha _ { 3 } , } \\ { \delta _ { 1 } = \delta _ { 1 } , } \\ { \hat { \sigma } _ { 2 } = G ( \alpha _ { 1 } , ~ \delta _ { 1 } , ~ \rho _ { 1 } , ~ \alpha _ { 3 } , ~ \delta _ { 3 } , ~ \rho _ { 3 } ; ~ t _ { 1 } , ~ t _ { 2 } , ~ t _ { 3 } ) , } \\ { \delta _ { 3 } = \delta _ { 3 } . } \end{array} \right. } \end{array}
$$

In this case tho cquations are reduced to two in the two unknowns ${ \pmb \rho } _ { \pmb 1 }$ and $\pmb { \rho _ { 3 } } ,$ and thcy also can be solved. This is the line of attack on the problom laid out by Lagrange in 1778, taken up independently and carricd out diffrently by Gauss in 1801, and followed more or lcss closely by many Iater writers. In spite of the hundreds of papers which have been wiitten on tho thcory of the deteimination of orbits,veiy httle that is really new or theoret" cally important has been added to the woik of Laplace and Gauss unless more than thiee obsei vations are used.

110.Preparation of the Observations. Whatevcr mclhod it may be pioposed lo follow, the obsei vations as obtained by the piactical astronomel requne col tain slghl coirections which should be made before the computation of the otbit is undcr taken

The attiactions of the moon and the sun upon thc cquatolial bulge of the carth causo a small peiodic oscillation and a slow secular changc in the position of the planc of its cqualorSince thc equinoxes aic the placcs wheie the cquator and ccliplic mterscct， the vernal cquinox undcigocs small periodic oscillations (the nutation） and slowly changcs its position along lhe ecliptc (thc piccession） It is obviously necessary to have all the observations referred to the same cooidimate system, and il is customaiy to use the mean equinox and position of the equator at the begmning of the yeai in which thc obsei vations are made.

Tho observed places aie also affected by the abciration of lght due to thc rovolution of the carth around the sun and to ils 1otation on its axis. Since the rolation is very slow compaied to thc rovolution，the aberration due to thc former is relatively small and generally may be ncglected, especially if the obsorvationg aro not very precise.

Supposo ${ \pmb { \alpha } } _ { \pmb { 0 } }$ and ${ \pmb { \delta } } _ { \pmb { \delta } }$ ale the observed right asconsion and declination of the body al any time Then tho right ascension and doclination referred to the mcan cqumox of the beginning of thc year,and corrected for the annual abciration, are

$$
\left\{ \begin{array} { l } { \alpha = \alpha _ { 0 } - 1 5 f - g \sin { ( G + \alpha _ { 0 } ) } \tan { \delta _ { 0 } } - h \sin { ( I I + \alpha _ { 0 } ) } \sin { \delta _ { 0 } } , } \\ { \delta = \delta _ { 0 } - \tau \cos { \delta _ { 0 } } - g \cos { ( G + \alpha _ { 0 } ) } - h \cos { ( I I + \alpha _ { 0 } ) } \sin { \delta _ { 0 } } , } \end{array} \right.
$$

Pfth Slep.The fifth stcp consists in determining the elements from the known positions of C at the times ti and tg. 140.Computation of the Time of Perihelion Passage. Tho methods of computing the time of perihelion passage depcnd upon whether the body moves in a parabola,ellipse,or hyperbola,and are based on the formulas of chap.v

Parabolic Case. Equation (32),of chap ${ \pmb v }$ ,is

$$
k ( t - T ) = \sqrt { 2 } q ^ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } \left[ \tan { \frac { v } { 2 } } + { \frac { 1 } { 3 } } \tan ^ { 3 } { \frac { v } { 2 } } \right] ,
$$

where $2 q = p$ Since $\pmb { u } = \pmb { v } + \pmb { \omega } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ and $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 }$ and $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { u } } _ { 3 }$ arc known this equation determines $\pmb { T }$

Ellptc Case.The first two equations of (49),chap.v., givo

$$
\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \sin \mathit { \mathcal { B } } = \frac { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } \sin \mathit { \vartheta } } { 1 + e \cos \mathit { \vartheta } } , } \\ { \cos \mathit { \mathcal { B } } = \frac { e + \cos \mathit { \vartheta } } { 1 + e \cos \mathit { \vartheta } } , } \end{array} \right.
$$

which uniquely define $\pmb { \mathit { 1 } } _ { }$ Then Kepler's equation

$$
{ \mathcal { M } } = n ( t - T ) = { \mathcal { B } } - \theta \sin { \it { \mathcal { B } } }
$$

determines $\pmb { \mathcal { T } }$ by using $\pmb { v }$ and the corresponding $\mathcal { B } \mathrm { a t } \mathrm { \ } t _ { \mathrm { i } } , \mathrm { \ } t _ { \mathrm { a } } , \mathrm { o r \ } t _ { 3 } .$ Hyperbohe Case The quantaty $\pmb { \bar { \imath } }$ is defined by

$$

Suppose the bodies aic homogencous m spherical layeis, then they will attiact (ach othel as though thcn masses wcie at then centers Let mi, $m _ { 2 } ,$ ,mn iepicscnt thel masgesLct thc cooidinates of m Icfclicd lo n fixed syslem of axes bc c., U,% (2 = 1， “ ,n) Let ",icpicscnl lhe distance betwecn the centeis of m. and m, Let h² iepiesenl a constant depcnding upon the units cmployed Thcn lhe components of force on mi paiallel to the -axis aie

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \mathfrak { - } \frac { \vec { k } ^ { 2 } m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } \mathfrak { r } _ { 1 , 2 } } \cdot \frac { ( \mathfrak { r } _ { 1 } - \mathfrak { x } _ { 2 } ) } { r \mathfrak { r } _ { 1 , 2 } } , \qquad \cdot , \qquad - \frac { \vec { k } ^ { 2 } m _ { 1 } m _ { n } } { r ^ { 2 } \mathfrak { r } _ { 1 , n } } \frac { ( \mathfrak { x } _ { 1 } - \mathfrak { x } _ { n } ) } { r \mathfrak { r } _ { 1 , n } } , } \end{array}

Theie aro similai cquations for cach body,and the whole system of cquations 1s

$$
\left\{ \begin{array} { l } { m _ { \star } \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } \bar { x } _ { 1 } } { d \bar { l } ^ { 2 } } = \mathrm { ~ - ~ } k ^ { 2 } m _ { \star } \displaystyle \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { j } \displaystyle \frac { ( \bar { x } _ { i } - \bar { x } _ { l } ) } { \bar { \iota } ^ { 3 } \bar { \iota } _ { 1 , j } } , } \\ { m _ { \star } \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } \bar { y } _ { 1 } } { d \bar { l } ^ { 2 } } = \mathrm { ~ - ~ } k ^ { 2 } m _ { \star } \displaystyle \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { j } \displaystyle \frac { ( y _ { i } - y _ { j } ) } { r ^ { 3 } \bar { \iota } _ { 1 , j } } , } \\ { m _ { \star } \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } \bar { z } _ { l } } { d \bar { l } ^ { 2 } } = \mathrm { ~ - ~ } k ^ { 2 } m _ { \star } \displaystyle \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { j } \displaystyle \frac { ( z _ { i } - z _ { j } ) } { r ^ { 3 } \bar { \iota } _ { 1 , j } } , \quad ( \mathrm { \iota } = 1 , \mathrm { ~ \iota } , \mathrm { ~ } , n , \mathrm { ~ } , \mathrm { ~ } \mathrm { ~ } ) + \mathrm { } ) . } \end{array} \right.

Each of thcse equations involves all of the ${ \mathfrak { z } } n$ variables $\mathfrak { L } _ { 1 } , \ y _ { 1 } ,$ and $\pmb { z } _ { \uparrow } ,$ and the systom must, therefore, bo solved simultaneously. Thero aro ${ \bf 3 } n$ cqualions cach of the socond order, so that the problom is of ordci $_ { 0 n }$

Equations (1) can bo put in a simplo and elegant form by the lntroduction of thc polcntial function, wluch in this problem will be denoted by $\pmb { U }$ instead of $\boldsymbol { \Vdash }$ . Thc constant $\mathcal { k } ^ { 2 }$ will be included in tho potcntial In chap.iv the potential, $\pmb { V }$ was defined by the integral $\displaystyle \boldsymbol { V } = \int \frac { d m } { \rho }$ In this case the system is composed of discrete masses,and the potential is

$$
\chi = { \textstyle \frac { 3 } { 2 } } \mathit { l c } ^ { 2 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } } { \tau _ { 1 , i } } , ( \tau \neq \Im ) .
$$

The partial derivative of U with iespect to x,1s

$$
\frac { \partial U } { \partial x _ { i } } = k ^ { 2 } m _ { 1 } \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { i } } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { m _ { j } } { r _ { 1 , j } } = \_ k ^ { 2 } m _ { 1 } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { j } \frac { ( x _ { i } - x _ { j } ) } { r ^ { 3 } , i _ { j } } ,
$$

and there aie gimilar equations in y and z. Therefoie cquations (1)can be written in thc form

$$

147.The Question of New Integrals. Ten of thc wholc 6n mtograls which arc icqured in oider to solve the pioblem completely have bccn found These ten intcgrals aic the only oncs known,and the qucstion arises whether any more of ceitain types exist.

In a piofound memoir in the Acta Mathematrca,vol. xi., Biung has domonstralcd that,whcn thc rectangular cooidinalcg aro chosen as dependent vaiiablcs, therc arc no ncw algebiaic mlegrals. This does not,of course, cxclude the possibility of algcbraic imtcgials when othel vauiables aie uscd． Pomcai6 has demonstraled in his piize memoir in the Acta Mathematica, vol xii ，and again with somc additions in Les Methodes Nouvelles de la Mecanique Celeste,chap V, that the Pioblem of Thice Bodics admils no ncw unform transcendental intcgials,evcn when the masses of lwo of the bodies arc vcry small compaied to that of Lhc thnd In lhis theorem the dependent vaiiables are tho elements of thc or bits of the bodies,which continually change undei thcir mutual atlractionsIt does not follow that mtegials of the class considered by Pomcaié da not cxist when other dependent vauiables are employed In fact, Levi-Civita has shown tho existencc of this class of integrals in $\mathfrak { a }$ special problcm，which comes under Poincare's theorem，whon suitablo varableg are uscd (Acta Mathematca, vol. xxx ). The practical importanco of ihe theorems of Bruns and Poincaré have often bcen overrated by those who havo forgotten the conditions under which they have been proved to hold true.

148. Transfer of the Origin to the Sun. Nothing is known of the absolute motions of thc plancts because thc observations fuinish imformation rcgadmg only thcir relalive positions,or ther posilions wilh icspcct to tho sun.It is truc that it is known that thc solai system is moving loward the constellation Iercules, but il must be icmcmbered that lhis motion is only with iespect to ceitain of thc slars The pioblom for the student of Cclestial Mechanics is to dolermine the relalive positions of tho members of the solar system；ol, in particular,to dctcrmine the positions of the planels with respect to the sun. To do this it is advantageous lo tansfer the origin to tho sun,and to employ the resulting differential cquations.

Suppose $m _ { n }$ is the sun and take its center as the origin,and let the coordinates of the body $m _ { \ell }$ 1eferred to thc new system be $x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , z _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ Then the old cooidinates are expiessed in terms of the now by the equations

$$
\mathfrak { c } = x _ { \nu } { } ^ { \prime } + x _ { n } , y _ { \ast } = y _ { \nu } { } ^ { \prime } + y _ { n } , z _ { \ast } = z _ { \nu } { } ^ { \prime } + z _ { \ast } , ( i \mathrm { = 1 } , \ \cdots , n \mathrm { - 1 } )
$$

Since the differences of the old variables are equal to the corresponding differences of the new,it follows that

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \begin{array} { c c c } { \displaystyle { \frac { \partial U } { \partial y _ { \ast } } = \frac { \partial U } { \partial y _ { \ast } } , \quad } } & { \displaystyle { \frac { \partial U } { \partial z _ { \ast } } = \frac { \partial U } { \partial z _ { \ast } ^ { \prime } } , \quad } } & { ( \ast = 1 , \mathrm { ~ \cdots ~ } , n - 1 ) , } \end{array} } \end{array}
$$

As a consequence of these transformations equations (3) become

$$

Tho first pail of tho chapter will be devoted to & discussion of somo of the pioportics of motion of an mfinitcsimal body when it is alliacted by two finite bodics which rovolve in ciicles around lhotr center of mass, and will mcludo the proof of the existence of cortam particular solutions in which the distances of the infiniteslmal body from tho finite bodies aie constantsThe second part of tho chaptor will bo dcvotcd to an exposition of a method of finding parlicular solutions of the molion of thiee finite bodies such lhul tho 1alios of their mulual distances aie constants These Bolutions include the foimer, but the discoveiable piopeities of molion aio so much fewol,and are obtaincd with so much moie diflicully， that il is advisablo to divide the discussion into two parts.

The mcthod adoptcd here is radically differont from that cmploycd by him,and lends itself much more readily lo a gonoializalion to the case wheie & larger number of bodicg is imvolvcd But,on the other hand, the reduction of the ordor of tho problom by one unit, which was a very imteresting feuluto of Lagrango's memoir, is not accomplished by this method IIowovor，as it has not bccn possiblo to make any use of this reduclion,it has not been of any practical importance.

attracled by finite masses but does not attiact them. 153.Jacobi's Integral. Equations (4) admit an integral whch was first given by Jacobi in Comptes Rendus de l'Academre des Scrences de Pars, vol. I11, $\mathrm { ~ \textbf ~ { ~ p ~ } ~ } 5 9$ ,and which has becn discussed by Hill in the first of his celebrated papers on the Lunar Theory， The Amercan Journal of Mathematics,vol 1., p.18,and again by Daiwin in h1s memoir on Periodic Orbits in Acta Mathemalica, vol. xx1., p. 102. $$
U = { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } ) + { \textstyle \frac { ( 1 - \mu ) } { r _ { 1 } } } + { \textstyle \frac { \mu } { r _ { 2 } } } ;

then equations (4) can be written in the form

$$
\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } - 2 \frac { d y } { d t } = \frac { \partial U } { \partial x } , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d t ^ { 2 } } + 2 \frac { d x } { d t } = \frac { \partial U } { \partial y } , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d t ^ { 2 } } \qquad = \frac { \partial U } { \partial z } } \end{array} \right.
$$

If these equations are multipled by $\mathbf { 2 } { \frac { d x } { d t } } , \mathbf { 2 } { \frac { d y } { d t } }$ and $\mathbf { 2 } { \frac { d z } { d t } }$ re-1 speotively,and added, the resulting equation oan be integrated,

since U is a function of a, y, and z alone, and give

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \left( \frac { d x } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { d y } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { d z } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } = V ^ { 2 } = 2 U - C } } \\ { { = u ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + \displaystyle \frac { 2 ( 1 - \mu ) } { \tilde { 1 } ^ { 1 } } + \frac { 2 \mu } { \eta _ { 2 } } - C . } } \end{array}
$$

156. The Regions of Real and Imaginary Velocity. Iaving detcrmmed thc forms of tho surfaces,it remains to find in what regions of relative space the motion is ieal and in what it is imaginaiy. The equation for the squaie of the velocity is

$$
V ^ { 2 } = x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + { \frac { 2 ( 1 - \mu ) } { r _ { 1 } } } + { \frac { 2 \mu } { r _ { 2 } } } - C .
$$

Suppose $\pmb { \mathscr { C } }$ is so large that the ovals and curtains are all eparatc.

Thc motion will be real in those portions of relative space for which the right member of this equation 1s positive If it is positivenonepoint ina closed fold itwillbe positive in every othor point within it, for the function changes sign only at a surface of zcro iclative velocity.

159. Tlsserand's Criterion for the Identity of Comets \* Comets Bonotimcs pass noar tho planots in their icvolutions around the sun,and thon thc cloinents of thoir orbits aro greatly changed. Tho planet Jupitcr is capecially potent in producing these perturbntions becauso of its groat mass and beeause at its distance tho nttrnction of tho sun is much less than it is at the distances of tho carth-like planela. Since a comct has no characteristic features by which it may bo rccognized with certainty,its identity might bc in quogtion if it were not followcd visually during the tumo of tho pa lurbalions.

Onc way of testing tho idontily of two comets appearing at difforenl epochs is lo take the orbit of the earlier and to compute tho porturbntions which it undcigocs,and then to compare the dorivod elomonts with thosc detormined from the later obser-\*Bulletn Aatronomque, vol vI, $\mathfrak { p } \ 2 8 \mathfrak { d } _ { 1 }$ and Mcc Cel,vol iv, $\mathfrak { p }$ 203.

vations,or,the start may be made with the elemcnts of the latei comet,and by inverse plocesses the earle1 elements may be computed and the compaiison made. One oi the other of theso plans has been followed untal recent years.

But the question ariseg if theie is not some relation among the elements which iemains unalteied by the pcrturbations This is the question which Tisserand has answeied im the affirmative in onc of his characteristically elegant and impoitant papeis on Celestial Mechanics.

Lct the eccentrcity of Jupitet's orbit be supposed cqual to zero, and the mass of the comet mfinitesimal While both of these assumptions are falso they are very nearly fulfilled,and the crror introduced will be inappreciable,especially as the comet will be near enough to Jupiter to suffer scnsible disturbances only & very short time Under these suppositions,and when the units are pioperly chosen, the integral

$$
\left( { \frac { d x } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( { \frac { d y } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( { \frac { d z } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } = x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + { \frac { 2 ( 1 - \mu ) } { r _ { 1 } } } + { \frac { 2 \mu } { r _ { 2 } } } - C
$$

holds true This is an answer to the question； for, when tho elements are known the velocity and coordinatcs can be computed at any timo,and the motion referred to rotating axes by equations (2) Hencc,to tcst the identity of two comets, compute the function (7) for each orbit and see if the constant C is tho same for both If the two values of C ate the same, the probability is very strong that only one comet has been observed;if they are diffeient.tho two comets are ccrtainly distinct bodics.

The process just explained has the inconvenience of involving considerable computation. This can bc largoly avoidcd by oxpressing (7) m terms of the ordinary celements of the orbit.Tho firsl step is to cxpress (7) in terms of cooidinates measurcd from fixed axesThe equations of transformation are the invorse of equations (2),viz,

$$

$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \left( \frac { d \mathfrak { c } } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { d y } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { d z } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } = \left( \frac { d \xi } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { d \eta } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { d \xi } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } } \\ & { \qquad + \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } - 2 \left( \xi \frac { d \eta } { d t } - \eta \frac { d \xi } { d t } \right) } \end{array}

$$

Let $\pmb { \mathscr { r } }$ represent the distance of the comet from the origm, and $\textit { i }$ the angle betwccn the plane of its instantancous orbit and the ${ \pmb \xi } _ { { \pmb \eta } } .$ planeThen cquations (24),Art 89, give

It will be noticed that tho elements of this formula are the instantaneous elements for motion around $\mathfrak { a }$ unit mass situated at the ccnter of mass of tho finite bodics, The actual elements used in Astronomy are tho clements rofcrred to the center of the sun, with the sun as thc attracting mass. 160.Stability of Particular Solutions. Five particular solutions of the motion of the infinitesimal body have been found. If the infinitesimal body is displaced $\mathfrak { a }$ very little from the cxact points of the solutions and givcn a small velocity il will either oscillate around these respective points,at Ieast for $\mathfrak { a }$ considerable time, or it will rapidly dcpait from themIn the first casc tho particular solution from which the displacement is made is said to bo stable; in the sccond case,it is said to be unstable.

The question of stability must be foimulatcd mathematically. Consider the equations

$$
\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } - 2 \frac { d y } { d t } = f ( x , y ) , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d t ^ { 2 } } + 2 \frac { d x } { d t } = g ( x , y ) } \end{array} \right.

$$

$$

where ${ \boldsymbol { \cdot } } { \boldsymbol { \cdot } } , ~ { \boldsymbol { y } } ^ { \prime } , { \frac { d { \boldsymbol { x } } ^ { \prime } } { d t } }$ and $\frac { d y ^ { \prime } } { d t }$ aie initially very gmall. On making these substitutions in (28),the diffrential equations become

$$

When the righl membeis are doveloped by Taylor's fornula, they tako the form

In the parlial detivatives $\textstyle { \mathfrak { r } } = { \mathfrak { x } } _ { 0 }$ and $y = y _ { 0 }$ Tho fist lorms in the iight membcrs aie icspcclively zero,henco cquiations (30) become

162.Particular Values of the Constants of Integration. Thc constants of integration will now be expressed in torms of the initial conditions,and it will be shown that the latter can be selected so that the motion will be periodic.

This is the equation of an ellipse with thc major and minor axcs lying along the cooidinate axes,and with the center at thc origin. Since $\lambda _ { 3 }$ is imaginary it follows fiom (43) and (44) that $c ^ { 2 } > 1$ ； therefore the major axis of the ellipse is parallel to the $\pmb { \eta }$ ax1s. which,for large values of ${ \mathfrak { c } } _ { \mathfrak { r } }$ is very near unity. The orbits havc the remalkable property that their cccontricity is independent of the imtial small displacements, dcpending only upon thc distribution of the mass bctwocn the finite bodies,and upon the one of the three straight line solutions from which they spiing.

It is obvious that this discussion is not complelely rigorous because the terms of higher degrec m the right members of the differontial equations have been neglected Thc lmcar lerms alone do not give sufficient conditions foi thc cxistencc of periodic orbits,and consequently when the discussion is thus restricted it answcrs only thc qyestion as to thc slability of the solution Bul in the present case periodic orbits actually cxist about all three points for all $0 < \mu \leq \frac { 1 } { 3 }$ Some spccial examples for $\mu = \frac { 1 } { 1 1 }$ wero found by Darwin n his memon in Acta Mathematica, vol 21. 163.Application to the Gegenschein. If tho constants $\textstyle \mathbb { K } _ { 1 }$ and $K _ { 2 }$ aic zelo the infinitesimal body will rovolve m an cllipso around the point of cquihbiium. If thcse conslanls arc not zero but small m numerical valuc compaicd to $\mathbb { K } _ { 3 }$ and $K _ { 1 , }$ tho motion will be nearly in an ollipse for a considerable timc,but will oventually depail very far from itIt would be possiblc to havo any number of infinilesimal bodics revolving around tho samo point without dislurbimg one anothcr.

Consider thc molion of thc earth around the sun. It is in a curve which is neaily a circle. Onc of the staaight line Bolulion points is exactly oppositc to thc sun,and if a meteor should pass near it wilh initial conditions appioximatoly such as havo bcen defincd in the last article it would make ono or moio circuits ai ound this point before pursuing its palh into olhel lcgions.If a vory great numbcr woro swaiming around this pomt at ono timc they would appoar from tho carlh as a hazy patch of light with ils ccnter at tho anti-sun,and elongated along thc ecliptic This is the appeaiance of tho gegenschein which was discovcicd indepondontly by Brorsen, Backhousc,and Bainard in 1855,1868,and 1875 respectively.

The crucial question scoms to be whethcr or not thore are onough moteors with ihe approximato initial conditions to oxplain tho obsorved phonomcna, but no ccitain answer can be givon Howcver,il is cerlain that tho metcors aio oxcccdmgly numcrous,as many as 8,ooo,ooo slriking into thc calh's atmospheie daily according to II. A. Ncwton；and it is only ronsonablo to suppose thal they cause lho zodiacal lighl which is vory brighl compared lo the gegonschein. Tho suggestion that lhus may bo tho causo of tho gogonschoin was firsl made by Gyldon in tlo closing paragraph of a mcmoir in tho Bulletn Astonomique, vol. 1., onltled,Sur un Cas Parlcuher du Pobleme dos Tos Corps.\*

165.Conditions for Circular Orbits. The thcolem of Lagiange that it is possible to stait thice fimtc bodies m such a manner that then oibits will be similai cllpses,all dcscibed m tho same time, will be piovcd n ths seclon It will bo cstablishcd fist for the special case in which the oibits aro cnclesIt will be assumed that lhe threc bodies aio piojected in the sainc plane Take tho ongin al thcir centei of mass and tho $\left. \xi \eta \right.$ plane as tho plane of motion Thon the difleiontial cqualions of motion aic (Art 143)

$$

On making tho substitution,and ieducmg as in Arl.1b2,it i found that

$$
\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } v _ { * } } { d l ^ { 2 } } - 2 n \frac { d y _ { i } } { d l } - n ^ { 2 } v _ { * } - \frac { 1 } { n _ { * } } \frac { \partial U } { \partial x _ { * } } = 0 , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } y _ { * } } { d l ^ { 2 } } + 2 n \frac { d v _ { i } } { d l } - n ^ { 2 } y _ { i } - \frac { 1 } { n _ { * } } \frac { \partial U } { \partial y _ { * } } = 0 . } \end{array} \right.
$$

If the bodics aro moving in circlcs auound tho origim wilh tho angular velocity $n _ { \uparrow }$ their coordmatcs with icspccl lo lhe iotating axcs aro conglants. Smce thc first and second derivativcs aro then zero, cquations (b4) becomo

$$
\left\{ { \begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle - n ^ { 2 } x _ { 1 } + k ^ { 2 } m _ { 2 } \frac { ( x _ { 1 } - \hat { x } _ { 2 } ) } { \bar { \eta } ^ { 3 } \bar { \lambda } _ { 1 , 1 } ^ { 2 } } + k ^ { 2 } m _ { \bar { \lambda } } \frac { ( x _ { 1 } - \hat { x } _ { 3 } ) } { \bar { \eta } ^ { 3 } \bar { \lambda } _ { 1 , 1 } ^ { 3 } } = 0 , } } \\ { { \displaystyle - n ^ { 2 } x _ { 2 } + k ^ { 2 } m _ { 1 } \frac { ( x _ { 2 } - x _ { 1 } ) } { r ^ { 3 } \bar { \lambda } _ { 1 , 1 } } + k ^ { 2 } m _ { 3 } \frac { ( x _ { 2 } - x _ { 3 } ) } { \bar { \eta } ^ { 3 } \bar { \lambda } _ { 2 , 3 } ^ { 3 } } = 0 , } } \\ { { \displaystyle - n ^ { 2 } x _ { 3 } + k ^ { 2 } m _ { 1 } \frac { ( x _ { 3 } - x _ { 1 } ) } { \bar { r } ^ { 3 } \bar { \lambda } _ { 1 , 3 } } + k ^ { 2 } m _ { 2 } \frac { ( x _ { 3 } - x _ { 2 } ) } { \bar { \eta } ^ { 3 } \bar { \lambda } _ { 3 , 3 } ^ { 3 } } = 0 , } } \end{array} } \right.

# CHAPTER IX.

# PERTURBATIONS-GEOMETRICAL CONSIDERATIONS

170.Meaning of Perturbations. Il was shown in chaptet V that if two spherical bodies move under thc influence of ther mutual atliaclions cach desciibcs & conic section with iespcct to their conter of mass asa focus,and that the path of each body with rcspecl to the other is a conieThe converse theoren is also truo;thal is,if the law of aicas holds and if the orbit of one body is a comc wilh rcspcct to the othcr as a focus,then if the force doponds only on the distance it varics inversely as the square of tho distancc (scc also Ait 58)If there is a resisting medium, or if oithcr of tho bodics is oblate, or if there is a thnd body attracting tho two under considoration, or if theie 1s any force acting upon thc bodics other than that of thc mutual attractions of the two sphcics, their orbits will ccase to be exact conic sections Supposo tho cooldmatcs and componcnts of velocity are given at a definito inslant to,thon,if tho conditions of the two-body poblem were picciscly fulfillcd, the orbits would be definite conics in which tho bodies would movc so as to fulfill the law of areas. Thc diffeionccs betwccn the coordinatcs and the components of vclocity in tho actual obits and those which the bodies would havo had if lho molion had bccn undisturbed aie the perturbatrons. It is ncccssary to includo the clangcs in the components of velocity as perturbalions, for the paths described depend not only upon the relativo positions of tho bodies and thc forces to which they aro subjcot, but also upon thc rolativo velocities with which they aro moving.

Several mothods of computing portuibations have been devised dopcnding upon tho somowhat differont points of vicew which may bo takon" Of these the two following are the ones most frequently uscd.

171.Variation of Cobrdinates. The simplest conception of porturbations is thal the codrdnates are direetly perturbedFor examplo, if $\clubsuit$ plancl is subject to the altiaction of another planet tho coordinatcs and componcnts of velooity of the former at any limo $\pmb { \ell }$ differ by dcfinitc amounts from what they would have been if the sun had been the only souice of attraction,and these difercnces are computed by appropriate devicesNo attempt is made to get the equations of the cuive described,and usually no general information as to what will happen in the coursc of a long time is securedThis method is appled only to comets and small planets.

172.Variation of the Elements. This mcthod is valiously called the Variaton of the Elements,the Varration of Parameter $\mathfrak { s } _ { 1 }$ and the Varraton of the Constants of Integiatron. Accoiding to this conception,a body subject to the law of giavitation is alwayg moving in& conio section, but in one which changes at each instant. The variable conic is tangent to the actual orbit at every point of it；and further,if the body were moving undisturbed in any one of the tangent conics it would have thc samc velocity at the point of tangency which it has in the actual oibit at that pont. This conic is said to osculate with the actual orbit at thc pont of contact The pertuibations are the differences betwcen the clcments of the orbit on the start,and those of tho osculating conic at any time An obvious advantage of this method is that the elements change very slowly，since in most of tho cascs which actually arise in the solar system the perturbing foiccs are small But if the perturbations were very large,as thoy aro in somo of the multiple star systems, this mcthod would lose its relative advantages.

Thc conception of perturbations as being variations of the clements arises quite natually m consideing the factois which dotcimine the eloments of an oibit It was shown in ohap.v. that the imitial positions of the two bodics and tho dicotions of plojection determine tho planc of the oibit,that tho imlial posltions and the velocitics of piojcction detcimine the length of tho majol axis,and that the imlial conditions,including lhc ducclion of projection and the veloctics, deterinme tho cccentricity and thc linc of the apsides

Tho body will bo moving in conc soctions which chango from lime lo time when it is subjcct lo the distuibmg impulses.Supposo tho instantancous impulses become very small,and thal thc mtorvals of timo betwoon them bccomo shorlcr and shortor. Tho goncral characteristics of thc motion will 1emain tho same. Al the lmil the impulses become a continually disturbing force,and tho or bil a conic section which continually changes
