174.Resolution of the Disturbing Force. Whatever may bo the source of thedsturbing foce it isconvenent,inodorto find its effeets upon the clements, to resolvo it into thiee rcclangular componentsIt is possble to do this in several ways,cach having advantageg fol paiticular puposcsThe onc will be adopted here whueh on the whole leads most simply to thc determination of the mannci in which the clements vaiy when thc body under consideialion 1s subject to any distuibing foreeIt would bo possible without much difficulty to deiive from geomctiical considci utions the cxpiessions fol the iates of change of the elements fo1 any distubmg foices, bul thc objcct of this chapter is to cxplain the natue and causes of pcrtuibations of various sotts, and the attention will not be divided by unnccessary digressions on melhods of computationThis pait falls natually to the mcthods of analysis, which will be givcn n the nexl chapter

Tho distubing foicc will be iesolved into thiee rectangular components. $( a )$ thc onthogonal component,\* $\textstyle { \mathfrak { S } } _ { i }$ wh1ch 1spe1- pendicular tothe planeof thoorbit,and which is taken positive whon dncctcd toward thc noilh pole of thc eclptic，(b） thc tangential componenl,T,which is in the lnc of the tangent,and which is tnken positivc when it acls in the dncclion of motion, and (c) tho noi mal component, $\pmb { N }$ ，which 1s perpendiculat to the tangenl,and wlich is taken positivo when duceted to the intelior of lhe orbit

Tho inslantancous cffocls of thcsc components upon the various cleinents will bo digcussed sepaiately，and, unless it is otherwise stated, it always must bc undeistood thal the iesults refei to the way in which thc clements are changmg at given instants, and nol to tho cuinulative cffccts of thc distuibing forces Although the effcots of the different compononts aic considered sepaiately,yet when two or more act simultancously it is sometimes necessiy to cstimalo somcwhat caiefully thc magntude of thei sepaiate porlurbalions,in ordor to dotomme tho charaoter of their jomt cffects.

IEFFECTS OF TIIB COMPONDNTS OF TII DISTURBING FORCE,

175.Disturbing Effects of the Orthogonal Component. In ordor to fix thc idens and abbreviate the language it wil be supposcd thnt tho disturbed body is the moon moving around the earth. The perluibations aiising from the distuibing action of tho sun are vcry gical and prcsent many featuies of exceptional intcrcst.Besidcs, this is tho case which Newton treated by mothods cssentially the samc as those cmploycd here t The \*A dcsignation duo to SirJohn Ioschel,Outhnes ofAtronomy,p 420. charactor of the perturbations alising fiom positive components alone will be investigated，in every case negative compononts change the elements in thc opposite way

It is at once evident that the orthogonal component will not change ${ a , e , T , }$ and $\omega ,$ 1f $\pmb { \omega }$ Is counted from a fixed hne n the plane of the orbit But the w in oidinary use is counted fiom tho ascending node of the oibit, hence if the negative of tho late of increase of $\otimes$ be multipled by cos $\textit { \textbf { \em } }$ tho iesult will be tho rato of inerease of ω due to the change in the origin from which il is reckoned Consequently it is sufficicnt to considcr thc changc8 in & and z when discussing the perturbations duc to the orthogonal component

185.Geometrical Resolution of the Disturbing Effects of a Third Body. The problem of the disturbance by a third body 1s much molc diffeull than thoso treated in Arts 183 and 184, because tho distubing force varies in a very complicaled manner.

![](images/95e37064c88ffeee058598aacce2220804f61100709dbc2f8f941fbf2e9ea5f3.jpg)

Supposo the threo bodics aic S,E,and m,and consider S as distuibmg tho motion of m around E. Two positions of m are shown at m and mz,and all the statcmonts which aie made apply fol both subscripts. Let EN icpiesenl m magnitudo and ducclion thc accoleration of S on E. The oidei of the lettcis indicates the dircction of the vcetol iepresenting tho foice, and the magnitude of tho veclor depends upon tho units employcd In the same units lol mK icprcsent m direction and amount the acccleration of S on m The veclor miK1 is gicatcr than EN because mjS 1s loss lhan ES,and mKa is less than EN because mzS 1s gicater than ES. By tho law of gravilation they aic pioporlional to the invorse squares of tho icspcotive dislanccs,

189.Precession of the Equinoxes. Nutation. Supposo tho largest sphere possible is cut out of thc earth leaving an equatorial ringEvery particle in this ring may bc considorcd as boing a small satellite； then, from the principles cxplancd in Arts. 186 and 186, the attiactions of the moon and sun will exorcisc disturbing accelerations upon them which will tond to shift thom with respect to the spherical coie. But thc particles of the ring are fastened to the solid eaith so that it partakes of any disturbance to which they may be subjcct. Since their combincd mass is very small compared to that of the sphorical body within them,and since the disturbing foices alc vcry slight, the changeg in the motion of the eaith will take placo vcry slowlv

Hrom the results of the last artcle it follows that the nodcs of the orbit of every particle will have a tendoncy to regrcss on the plane of the disturbing body The angle bctwcon the plane of the moon's orbit and that of the ecliptic may be neglectcd for tho moment as it is small compared to the inclination of thc oarth's equator.They communicate this tendency to the whole earth so that the plane of the eaith's cquator tuins in tho ietiogiade du cetion on thc plano of tho cclipticOn the other hand,it follows fiom the symmetiy of the figuic with 1espect to the nodes of the Obits of the parlicles of the cquatoiial ing that theic will be no change m thc inchnalion of the plane of the equatol to that of tho ccliplic or thc moon's oibitThe mass moved 1s so great, and thc foices aeting are so sinall, that this ret1ograde motion, called thc pccesson of the equnoces，amounts to only about 50"2 annually,ol, tho plane of the carth's equator makes a revolution mabout 26,000 yeais.

The moon is very near to tho earlh compaied to the sun,and the orthogonal componont aiising fiom its atliaction is greatei than that commg from the sun's attraction， The main regiession is, therefore,on the moon's orbit,which is inclined to the eclptio about 5° 9'Sincc the lne of the moon's nodes makes a revolution in about 19 yeais, thc plano with iespect to which the equator rogresss performs a revolution n the same time.This produces $\mathbf { a }$ slight noddng in the motion of the poleof thecquator around tho pole of tho ecliptic,and is called mtation

Tho quantilativo agicement between theory and observation of lhe rato of picccssion provcs that the equatoiial bulge is soldly attaehcd to thc icimainder of tho eaith. If the earth were a relatively lhin solid crust floating on a liqund interior,as was once supposcd,it would probably slde somewhat on the interior and givo a moro rapid prcccssion. 1

In the orbit of the moon e is approximately equal to , and consequently a good idea of the numerical magnitudes of Tand N and the circumstances under which thoy change sign can be obtained by neglecting thosc terms which have e as a factor. 193.The Annual Equation. Since the orbit of tho earth is an ellipse tho distancc of the sun undeigoes considerablc vaiiations, Thc farther the sun is from the eauth the feeblci aie its distuibing effects,and in particulai， the power of lengthening tho month considercd m the preceding al ticle Theieforc,as tho carth moves from perihelion to apholon the distui bance which sncreases the length of the month will become less and less; thal is,the length of the month will become shortcr,ol the moon's angular motion will be accelerated． While thc carth is moving fiom aphelion to pelihelion the moon's motion will, for the opposite reason,be retarded. This is the Annual Equation amounting to a ltle mole than 11',and was discovered from observations by Tycho Brahe about 1590.

195. The Variation. There is another important porturbation in the motion of the moon which does not depend upon lhe eccentricity of its orbit,It was discovered by Tycho Brahc, from observation,about 1590. Newton explamed the cause of it in thc Prneipia by a direct and clegant method which elicitcd the prase of Laplace

It can be explained most rcadily by supposing that the undisturbed motion of the moon is in $\mathfrak { a }$ cirele As has bccn shown the normal component of the sun's disturbing acccleration 1s negativo in the intervals $\pmb { m } _ { 8 } m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 }$ and $m _ { 4 } m _ { 5 } m _ { 0 } ,$ with maximum values at $\pmb { m _ { 1 } }$ and $m _ { 5 }$ Suppose the undisturbed motion at $\pmb { m _ { 1 } }$ is in $\mathfrak { a }$ circle, that is,that the acceleration due to the attraction of the carth exactly balances the centrifugal acceleration Thcro is no tangential component at this point but a large negative noimal componentThe 1csult is that the force which tends toward $\pmb { \mathcal { B } }$ 1s diminished and the orbit is less curved at this point than the cltoleTheiefore thc moon will recede to a gtcater distance from the carth in quadratuie than m the circular orbit. At the point $\pmb { m } _ { 3 }$ thc tangential component is zero,the force which tendg towaid $\pmb { \mathcal { L } }$ Is increascd,and the curvature is grcater than in the circle The conditions vaiy continuously fiom those at $m _ { 1 }$ to those at $m _ { 8 }$ in tho intorval $\pmb { \mathscr { m } } _ { \pmb { \imath } } \pmb { \mathscr { n } } _ { \pmb { \imath } }$ The coiresponding changes in the remaindei of tho orbit aic evidenl. Tho wholo icsult is that the oibil is lenglhencd m the direction perpendicular' to the ling from tho carth lo the sun. If tlo sun is assuned to be so far distant that its disturbing effccts in thc mterval $\pmb { \mathscr { m } } _ { 3 } \pmb { \mathscr { m } } _ { 5 } \pmb { \mathscr { m } } _ { 7 }$ aro cqual to thosc in tho inteival $m _ { n } n n _ { 1 } n _ { 2 }$ ，the orbil, under proper mtral conditions, 1s symmotiical with respcct to $\pmb { \mathcal { L } }$ as a centei, and closely resombles an cllipse in form. This chango of form of the orbit,and tho auxiliary changes in tho ialo nt which tho radiug vector swceps over arcas, givo rise to an mcqualily in longiludo between the mcan position and the truo position of tho moon which amounts at timcs to about $3 9 ^ { \prime } 3 0 ^ { \prime \prime }$ and is called tho varatron.

The variation hns an mlcicsting and important conncclion with the modein mothods in the Lunar Thcory， which were foundcd by G W.IIillin his celebraled mcmoirs in iho fist volume of the American Journal of Malhematics,and in tho Acta Mathematica,vol. vil. A complote account of this mothod is given in Brown's Lunar Theory m tho chaplor entitled,Method wth Rectangular CoordunatesHll negleeted th solarpaialax,that is, ho assumed that the disturbing foice is equal im colicspondimg points in conjunetion with,and opposition to, the sun Instead of takmg an ellpse as a first approximation, he took as an inle1- mediate oibit that vasratronal obit which is closcd with icspect to axes rotating with the mean angular velocity of the sun, with ${ \mathfrak { a } }$ synodic period equal to the synodic period of tho moon Tho conception is not only one of gieat value,bul tho analysis was made by Hill with rare ingenunty and elegancc.

The lne of apsidcs of the moon's orbit makes a complete revolution in about $9 \frac { 1 } { 2 }$ ycars.

But it was shown in Art 181 that & given noiinal component is moro offectivo in changing the cccentricity when tho moon is neal apogec than it is when the moon is correspondingly ncar perigec.Besrlcs this, since N is proportional to r,as follows fiom lho sccond cquation of (18), the noimal componcnt is lauger the grcalor lho moon's distancoFor both of thcse rcasons,while lho moon is in the arc mzma the incroase of tho cccentricity with tho perigce at mg is gieatcr than thc decreaso with thc perigeo at mgThc two cascs combmed give & small second order rosidual inereaso in the cccentiicity which may be represontcd by +△ie. Similarly,whle thc moon 1s in the region mmg tle effccts of tho normal component on the cccentricily with the poligco al mz and mo are respectively an ineroasc and a dccrcasc. On paymg hecd to thc relativo posttions of the apogec,il 1s scen thal tho combined cffcct on the eccentricity is a second ordcr residual incrcaso +△ze. By analogous discussions, the combined cffects for tho moon in the arcs mgmg and mgm ale the positivc second order residuals +△ae and +△4e

The question ariscs whethor tho second oidor residuals are not in some way destroyed In order to show that they also vanish consider the case m which the line of apsides has a symmetrically opposite position with respect to the line ${ \overline { { B } } } { \tilde { S } } _ { \varepsilon }$ that is,the case in which the pergee Is at $\pmb { m } _ { 8 }$ or $m _ { 4 }$ When the perigce is at $m _ { 4 }$ and the moon in the region $m _ { 2 } m _ { 4 }$ the cccentricity is increased by tho normal component,when the perigee is at $m _ { 8 } ,$ tho cccontricity 1s decreased． The decrease in the latter case is gieater than the increase in the former because when the perigee is at $\pmb { m } _ { 8 }$ thc region $m _ { 2 } m _ { 4 }$ Is near the apogee Theiefole the combincd cftcct is $\mathfrak { a }$ second order decrease in the eccenticity；and, snce the aio $m _ { 2 } m _ { 4 }$ Is not only situated the same relatively with respcct to thc earth and sun but also with respect to the moon's oibit as whon the line of apsides was the lne $m _ { 2 } m _ { \mathfrak { d } _ { 1 } }$ it follows that the sccond order decrease in the eccenlricity is $- \Delta _ { 1 } e$ It is found similarly that when the moon is in the arcs $m _ { 4 } m _ { 0 } , \ m _ { 0 } m _ { 8 } ,$ and $\textmu m _ { 8 } m _ { 2 }$ the sumg of the changes of the cccentricity when the perigce is at $m _ { 4 }$ and $m g$ are respectively $- \ \Delta _ { 2 } e , \ - \ \Delta _ { 3 } e ,$ and $- \Delta _ { 4 } e$ Whcn these socond order residuals are added to those obtained when the line of apsides was the line $m _ { 2 } m _ { 8 }$ the result is zero，A coircsponding discussion leads to the same results for any other position of the line of apsides,viz., It can be paired with another which is symmetrically opposite with respect to the line $\mathcal { B } \mathbb { S }$ so that when the perigee is taken m both directions on each line the total effect of the normal component on the eccentricity is zeio. Therefore the normal component sn the long run makes no permanent change in the eccentrcity of the moon's orbrt, and a somewhat similar discussion establshes the same resull for thc tangential component,

The sun docs not,however,stand still whilo the moon makcs its revolution, and the conditions which have been assumcd are nevei exactly fulfilled. Novertheless, it is uscful to show how the different configurations,even though changng ftom instant to instant,may be paied. In a very grcat number of revolutions thc supplementary configurations will have oecuircd an cqual number of times,and the eocentricity will have returncd to its original value The period 1cquired for this cycle of change depends in the first place upon the peiiods of the sun and the moon,in the sccond place,upon the eccentricity of the sun's orbit (the earth's orbit)； and lastly,upon the manner in which the lncs of apsides of the sun's and moon's orbits rotate.

The present system, with abundant geological and biological evidence ol a very long existence for lhe carth in at least approximately its present condition, shows with reasonable certainly that the system is ncaily stable,if not quite It is an interesting fact, though,that those two clemcnts,the linc of nodes and the line of apsides, which may change contmually in one direction without threatemng the stabilty of the system do,on the average,iespectively ietiograde and advance foever

200.The Evection. It has just been shown that the cccentiicity does not change in the long iun;yel it undeigocs peiiordic vaiiations of considciable magnitude which give iisc to tho lai gest lumar peituibation, known as the evection Al its maximum cffcct it displaccs the moon in geocentiic longitude through an anglo of about 1°15' compaied lo its position in thc undisturbcd elliptic o1bit This variation was discovered by Iipparchus and was caiefully observed by Ptolemy.

The porturbations of the eloments,and of the cccentricity in parlicular, depend upon two things, the position of the moon in its orbit,and tho position of the moon with icspect to thc earth and sun. Suppose tho moon and sun starl in conjunction with the perigce at m1 Consider the motion throughout ono synodic revolution. It follows from thc Table of Ait 182 and Figs 57 and 58 that the cccentricity is nol changing whcn tho moon is at mi；that it is dccieasing, or zcro,whcn the moon is al mz, m, and m4, that it is not changing when the moon is at mb;that it is mcreasing,or zero,whcn the moon is al mo, m,and mg； and that it ceases to chango when tho moon has roturned to mi again. This is tiue only under the hypolhcsis thal tho perigce has rcmained at mt lhroughout the wholc rovolution;or,in othcr words, that the line of apsidcs advances as fast as tho sun moves in its orbit. Now, tho actual caso is that the sun moves about 8b timcs as fast as the line of apsides rotalesSinco tho synodio period of the moon is about 29 5 dayg while tho sun movcs about one degreo daily,tho moon will be ahout 20° past its perigee whon it arrives at miWhat modification in tho conclusions does this introduce? The normal component is nogalive and, in this part of the orbit， causes an ineroaso in thc cccentricity，whilo tho tangential makes no chango, since it is zcro. As the moon proceeds past mi the normal componcnl becomes less in numcrical valuc, while the tangential component becomcs negativo and lends to decroase the cccentricity.“The tendoncies of the two components to change the cccentricily in opposito directions balanco when tho moon is at somo point botwecn m1 and mz, instcad of al mi,after which the eccentiicity decieascsTheie is & coriesponding advance of the point near mg at which the cccentiicity ceases to decioase and begins to incrcase Similar conclusions are reached stai ting from any other initial configuiation,

The results may be summaiized thus The perturbatrons of the sun decrease thc eccentricity of the moon's orbil somewhat more than half of a synodical ievolution,and thon inciease it for an cqual time. These changes m tho ecenliicity cause dcviations in the geocentric longitude from the ones given by the cllptic theory,which constitute the eveclhon. The appiopriate methods show that the period of this inequality is aboul 31 8 days.

201.Gauss’ Method of Computing Secular Variations. It has been shown in the piecedmg articles that some of thc elements, such as the line of nodcs and the line of apsides,vaiy in one direction without lmit. This change is not at a uniform rato, for in addition to the general variations, there aie many short period Oscillations which aio of such magnitude that tho elcmcnl frcquently varies in tho opposite direction. When the iesults are put into the symbois of analysis, the general avcrage advanco 18 represented bya term proportional to the timc,callcd tho secular varaton， while the deviations from this uniform chango aio represented by a sum of periodic lorms having various periods and phasegThus it is seen that the secular vaiiations aro oauscd by & sort of average of the distuibing forces when thc disturbing and disturbed bodres occupy every possiblo position with rcspect to each other

There are other elements,such as the inolination and tho eocentricity which， though periodic in the long iun， vaiy continuously in onc direction on the average for many thousands of years These changes may be rcgarded ag secular variar tiong also,and they likewise result from & sort of avcrage of perturbations.

In 1818 Gausg published a memoir upon tho theory of secular variations based upon the conceptions just outlined. Iis method has been appled especially in the computation of tho secular vauations of thc elements of tho planctary orbitsInstcad of consideiing the molions of the bodics, Gauss supposed that tho mass of cach planet is sprcad out in an clhptical iing coinciding With its oibit m such a mannor that the densily al each poinl is inversely as the velocity with which the body moves at that pomt He then showed how to compulc tho attraction of ono ring unon the othcr,and the rate at which thoir positions and shapes change under the influcnce of these foceg

The method of Gauss has been the subject of quite a number of memoirsPiobably the most useful fol piactical purposes is by G W IIill in vol I of thc Astionomecal Papers of the Amercan Ephemeris and Nautical Almanac Iill's foimulas have becn applied by Professor Euc Doolittle with gicat success, the iesults which he obtaincd agrccing vciy closely with those found by Leveilici and Newcomb by cntely diffeicnt methods.

202.The Long Period Inequalitles. In the theories of the mutual peitubations of thc planels vciy laige teims of long periods occulThey aise only when thu periods of the two bodies considercd aic neaily commensuiable, and it is casy to discover then causc from geomctrical considerations

Since the most imporlant variation occurs in the mutual pertuibations of Jupiter and Saturn the explanation will be adapted to that casc. Five timcs the pcriod of Jupitei 1s a lttle more than twicc tho period of Saluin Suppose that the two planets ac in conjunction at lhe oiigin of timc on the lino lo. After five revolutions of Jupiter and two of Saturn thcy will bo in conjunction agam on a lne l vory ncar lo, but havmg a littlo gicater longitudo This continues indofiniloly,cach conjunction occuiring at a htlle gieatel longitudo than the proccding Conjunctions occurring frequcntly at about the samo pomls in tho orbits causo very large perturbations,and tho Long Perod is tho limo which it takcs the point of conjunction to mako a complcto revolution. In tho case of Jupiter and Saturn it is about 918 years. This mequality, which Is the greatest in the longitudes of the planets, displacing Jupiter $\Omega \mathbb { 1 ^ { \prime } }$ and Saturn $\nleq 9 ^ { \prime }$ ,long baffled astronomers in thcn attempts to explain it as a necessary consequcnce of the law of giavitation. Laplace finally made onc of his many impoitant conlnbutions to Celcstial Mechanics by pointing out its truc cause, and showing that theory and observation agree

# CHAPTER X.

# PERTURBATIONS-ANALYTICAL METHOD.

203.Introductory Remarks. The subject of the mutual pertui bations of the motions of the heavenly bodies has been one to which many of the great mathematicians, from Newton's time on,have devoted a great dcal of attention. It is needless to say that the problem is very diffeult and that many mothods of attacking it have been devised. Since the geneial solulions of the problem have not been obtained it has been necessary to ti cat special classes of perturbations by special methodsIt has becn found convenient to divide the cases which arisc in the solar system into three geneial classes, (a) the Lunar Theoly and satelhte theories,(b) the mutual pei turbations of the planets,and (c) the perturbations of comets by planetsThe method which will be given in this chapter is applicable to the planetary theories,and it will be shown n the proper places why il is nol applicable to the other cases， References were given in the Iast chaptci to treatises on the Lunar Theory,especially to those of Tisserand and Brown, Some hints will be given in this chapter on the method of computing the pertuibations of comets.

The chief difficultics which arise in getting an understanding ot the theories of perturbations come from the laige number of variables which it 1s necessary to usc， and the very long transformations which must be made,in oder to put thc equations in & foim suitable for numerical computations. It is not possible, because of the lack of spaco, to develop heie m detail tho explicit expressions adapted to computation；and,mdecd,it is not desired to emphasize this part, fol it is much more important to get an accurate understandng of the nature of the pioblem, thc mathematcal features of the methods employed, the lmitations whch ae necessary, the exact places wheie approximalions aie intioduced,if at all,and their character,the ouigin of the vaiious sorts of terms,and the foundations upon which the celebiated theoicms regarding the stabilty of the solai system rest

Thero are two general methods of considering perlurbations, (a) as the variations of the coordinates of tho various bodies.

and (b) as the variations of the elements of their oibits. These two conceptions weie explaned in the beginning of the preceding chapterThcir analytical development was begun by Eulei and Clanaut and was caiied to a high degiee of perfection by Lagrange and Laplace,Yet thcie were pomts at which pure assumpttons weie inade, it having become possible to establish completcly the legitimacy of the procecdings，undel the proper iestrctions,only durng the lattei half of the nneteenth century by the aid of the woik in pure Mathemalics of Cauchy, Weierstrass, and Poincai6

204.Illustrative Example.Tho mathematical basis for the theory of pertuibations is oftcn obscuicd by the large number of variables and tho compleated foimulas which must be used. Many of the cssential fealurcs of tho method of computing pertui bations can be illustiatcd by sunplor examples which aie not subjcct to tho complexilics of many vaiiables and mvolved formulas.Onc will bo sclccted in which the physieal relations are also simplo

Consider the solution of

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } v } { d l ^ { 2 } } + h ^ { 2 } v = - \mu \left( \frac { d v } { d l } \right) ^ { 3 } + \nu \cos l t ,
$$

where k²,μ, v,and laio positivc constantsIf μ and y were zero tho differcntial cqualion would be that which defines simple harmonc motion. Il arises in many physical problems, such as that of the simplo pcndulum,and of all classes of musical inslruments In order to mako thc mterprolation defimle, supposc it bolongs to thc problom of lho vibiations of a tunmg fork．The fitst teim in tle right membel may bo interpreted as being due to the rosistance of lho mcdium m which tho tuning fork vibrates It is not assor lcd, of couise, that tho iesistance to tho vibrations of a tunmg fork varics as the third power of the velocityAn odd power is takcn so ihal tho differential cquation will have the same foim whether the molion i in thc posilivo or negative direction,and the firsl powor is nol laken becauso then the differontial cquation would bc lincar and could be completely integiated in finito torms withoul any dimculty

Thc loft member of cquation (1） will bo considered as defining the undisturbed molion of the tuning fork. Tho firsl term on the right introduccs a pcrturbation which depends upon the velocity of the tuning fork, the second teim on the iight introduces a perturbation which is independont of the position and velocity of the tuning foik The first is analogous to the mutual perturbations of the planets,which depend upon thcir relative positions,the second is more of the natuie of the foices which produco the tides,for they are exterior to the carth．The tides are defined by equations analogous to (1)

In order to have cquation (1) in the form of tho equations which arise in the thcory of perturbations, Iet

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \mathfrak { x } = { \mathfrak { x } } _ { 1 } , \ } & { { } \ \frac { d { \mathfrak { x } } } { d t } = { \mathfrak { x } } _ { 2 } . } \end{array}
$$

Then (1) becomes

$$
\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \displaystyle \frac { d x _ { 1 } } { d t } - x _ { 3 } = 0 , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d x _ { 3 } } { d t } + k ^ { 2 } x _ { 1 } = - \omega _ { 3 } ^ { 3 } + \nu \cos { \it \mathcal { U } } . } \end{array} \right.
$$

The corresponding diffeential equations for undisturbed motion are

$$

$$

Consider cquations (10)．The right member of each of them has n term which contains lonly as a simple factor,while elsewhere $\pmb { \ell }$ appears only in the sine and cosine terms.The terms which are proportional to $\pmb { \ell }$ seem to indicate that $\pmb { \alpha }$ and $\beta$ increase or deorcase indofinitcely with the time；but it must be remembered that equations (10) are only approximate expressions for α and β, which are useful only for $\mathfrak { a }$ limited time.It might be that the rigorous expressions would contain higher powerg of $\mathbf { f } ,$ and that tho sums would havo bounded values, just as

$$

is an oxpression whog numerical value does not exceed unity, though $\mathfrak { a }$ consideration of the first term alone would lead to the conclusion that it becomes indefinitely great with t. On the othcr hand tlio prcsenco of terms which increase proportionally to the timo may indicatc an actual indefinite ·increase of the clemonts $\pmb { \alpha }$ and $\beta$ For oxample, it was found in·the preceding chaptcr that the lino of nodes and the apsides of the moon's orbit respootively rcgrcss and advance continually. The terms which change proportionally to $\pmb { \ell }$ nrc called secular terms.

The right mombers of cquations (10) also contain periodic terms having tho periods and $\frac { 2 \pi } { l - k }$ These are known as periodic lerms. If land h are noarly equal the terms which involvo sines or cogines of (l-k)t have very long periods,and are called long period lerms, Sometimes torms arise which are the products of l and poriodic torms. These mixed terms are called Poisson terins bocauso thoy wero cncountered by Poisson in the discussion of the variations of thc major axes of the planetary orbits. If (10) are substitutcd in (b) tho resulting expressions for x1 and x2 contain Poisson terms but no secular terms.

As has bcen said, the solutions cxpressed as power seies in the masses converge if the interval of time is taken not too gieat In a general way, the smaller the masses of the planets the longer the time during which the series converge. In the Lunar Thcoiy the sun plays the role of the distuibing planetSimce its mass is very gleat compared to that of the central body,the earth,the serics in powers of the masses as given above would convcrgc for only $\mathfrak { a }$ veiy shoit time,probably only a few months instead of years. Such a Lunar Thooiy would be entirely unsatisfactoiy. On this account the perturbations in the Lunar Theory aro developed in powcls of the ratio of the distances of the moon and the sun from the eaith,and special artifices are cmploycd to avoid secular terms inallthe elements except the nodes and perigee.

Any system of differential equations of the form (38) is known asa canonical system,and thcy possess properties which make them particulaily valuable in theoictical investigationsThcie is $\mathfrak { a }$ theorem that any dynamical pioblem in which the foices can bc represented as pal tial derivatives of a potential function can be eYpressed in this form，and if it is possible to put & problcm in tho canonical form it 1s possible to do so in infinitely many systems of dependent variables

If equations (38) were solved they would give thc values of thc coordinates at $\scriptstyle t _ { 0 }$ which would have to be used to obtain the true coordinates at the time $\mathbf { \delta } _ { t , \tiny { \begin{array} { r l } \end{array} } } t _ { \star }$ under the supposition that thc planet moved in an undisturbed ellpse during $t - t _ { 0 }$ 、If the variablcs were the elliptic elements the solutions of the equations would give the elements which would have to be uscd to compute tho cobrdinates at the time $\ell ,$ when they are supposed to have been constant duimg the interval $t - t _ { 0 }$ Thus,when the elements have been found the remainder of the computation is that of undisturbed motion

214.Transformation to the Ordinary Elements. On carrymg out tho complcated computations of $\frac { \partial \mathscr { s } } { \partial \mathscr { v } _ { 0 } } , \qquad , \frac { \partial \mathscr { s } } { \partial \mathscr { z } _ { 0 } }$ by means of the cquations given in Aits.86,87,and 88,and cx" pressing all the pai tial derivatives in terms of the ncw variables, the partial derivatives $\frac { \partial { R _ { 1 , ~ 2 } } } { \partial { { \lambda } _ { 0 } } }$ ， $\underbrace { \partial R _ { 1 , \ 2 } } _ { \partial z _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } } \underbrace { \partial \mathrm { r e } } _ { \partial \mathrm { t r e } }$ found in terms of the elements &ad $\begin{array} { r } { \frac { \partial \ R _ { 1 , ~ 2 } } { \partial \otimes } , \quad \cdots , \ \frac { \partial \ R _ { 1 , ~ 2 } } { \partial \epsilon } . } \end{array}$ On substituting in (40) and expressing $\frac { \partial f } { \partial \mathfrak { x } _ { 0 } } , \cdot \mathbf { \nabla } \cdot , \frac { \partial { f } } { \partial \mathfrak { z } _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } } \ln$ te1ms of the clments, $\frac { d \vartheta } { d t }$ is found in terms of tho elcments and tho derivatives of the peiturbative function, $\mathcal { R } _ { 1 , ~ 2 } ,$ with respcct to tho olemonts.

215.Method of Direct Computation of Lagrange's Brackets. The transformations required in the method of the preceding article are vory laborious,and the dirccl computation of the brackets, though considorably involvcd,is to be preferred from a practical point of view.Al of the computalion in the transformatrons of ths sort mght be avoided by using canoncal varrables; but,in order to employ them, a longthy digression upon the properties of canonical systems would bc necessaiy,and such & discugsion 1s outside the lmits of thg work. Stll, the labor may be nolably reduced by first taking elomonts somewhat diffcient fiom those defined in chapter v，and then transforming to thosc in more ordinary use. The following is based on Tisserand's exposition of Lagrange's method.\*

\*Tisgerand's Mecamque Celeste,vol I,p.170.

Let the xy-plane be the plane of the ecliptic, $\Omega \mathcal { P }$ the projection of the orbit upon the celeslial sphere, $\pi$ the projection of the perihelion pont,and $\mathcal { P }$ the piojeclion of the position of the planet at the time $\pmb { t }$ In place of $\pmb { \pi }$ and $\epsilon ,$ adopt the new elements $\pmb { \omega }$ and $\pmb { \sigma }$ defined by tho equations

$$
\left\{ { \begin{array} { l } { \omega = \pi - \Omega , } \\ { \sigma = - n T } \end{array} } \right.
$$

223. (b) Development of the Coefcients in powers of e1 and e2: The radu rand rz vary from a(1 - ei) and az(1 - ez) to a1(1 + e) and az(1 + e2) respectively. Let

The angles ${ \pmb u } _ { 1 }$ and ${ \pmb u } _ { \mathbf { 2 } }$ aic expicsscd in teims of the tiuc anomalics, U1and vz,and the elements by (82)The true anomalies are equal to the mean anomalics plus the equations of the center, which may be denoted by wl and waLot l and l iepicsent the mean longitudcs counted fiom the x-axis [Fig (62)], then

It follows from (81) that Ri,a can be written in the form

$$
R _ { 1 , ~ 2 } = \bar { F } [ a _ { 1 } ( 1 + \rho _ { 1 } ) , a _ { 2 } ( 1 + \rho _ { 2 } ) ] ,
$$

where $\pmb { \mathscr { F } }$ is a homogeneous function of ${ { \mathfrak { a } } _ { 1 } }$ and ${ \pmb q } _ { \pmb { \mathrm { = } } }$ of degiee -1. Therefore

$$
R _ { 1 , 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 1 + \rho _ { 2 } } R \left[ a _ { 1 } + a _ { 1 } \frac { \rho _ { 1 } - \rho _ { 2 } } { 1 + \rho _ { 2 } } , a _ { 2 } \right] .
$$

The right member of this cquation can bc developed by Tayloi's formula, giving

$$

229. Lagrange's Treatment of the Secular Variations. The prescnce of the secular terms in the expressions for the clements seems to indicate that, if it is assumed that the series reprcsent the elements for all values of the time, then the elements change without limit with the time. But this conclusion is by no means necessarily true. For example, consider the function

$$
\sin { \left( c m t \right) } = c m t - \frac { c ^ { 3 } m ^ { 3 } t ^ { 3 } } { 3 ! } + \cdot ,
$$

where c ig & constant and m & very small factor whiqh may take the place of $\mathbf { \widetilde { a } }$ massThe series in the right member converges for all values of t. This function is never greater than unity for any value of the time, yet if its expansion in powers of m were given, and if the first few terms were considered without the law of the coefficients being known,it might seem that the series represents a function which increascs indefinitely in numerical value with the time.

On following out the idea that the seoular terms may be expansions of functions which ale always finite, Lagiange has shown (scc Collected Wmks,vols v and v1),undei certain assumptions which have not bcen logically juglified,that the secular teims aiQ m ieality the cpansions of petiodic teims of very long period Thcse teims difer fiom the long peiiod vaiiations (Art 226) in that they come fiom the small uncompensatcd paits of the peiiodic variations, mstead of dncctly from special conditions of conjunctionsAs a iule these icims aie vcry sinall,and then periods aie much longei than thosc of the sensible long peiiod teimsIt will not be possiblo lo give here moie than a veiy geneial idca of the method of Lagiange

$$

whero ei,π, ctc.,are thc cloments of tho orbit of m,,and l is a now vaiiablo not to bo confugcd with tho mean longiude. Theie is a second icason why the results obtained by mcchanical quadatues may not be sufficicntly cxact It has so far bccn assumed thatf(t) isa function of t alone,oi,in othel woids, thal tho elemcnts of the oibits on whch it depends aic constants This is thc assumption in computing peituibations of tho fiist oiderIf it 1s not cxact enough,ncw valucs of $f _ { \theta } ( t _ { 1 } )$ ，， $f _ { \theta } ( t _ { n } )$ can be computcd, on using im them thc icspectivc valucs of the cleinents s which welc found by thc fisl imtcgiation From thc ncw valucs of $f _ { \mathfrak { g } } ( t _ { 1 } )$ 、， $\int _ { \theta } \bigl ( t _ { n } \bigr )$ a mole appioximate valuo of the integral can bc obtained. The piocess of computing pcituibations by thc mothod of mechanical quadiatuies, as compaicd with that of using the expanded form of the pei tuibalivc funetion, has ils advantages and its dsadvanlages. It is an advaniage that in cmploymg mechancal quadialuies it is not ncccssaiy lo cxpicss tho peiturbing forces explicilly m teims of the clcmcnts and thc lime This is somelimes of great importancc,for,in cases wheio the eccentiieities and inclnations aie laigc,as in some of tho astcroid orbits,these cxpiessions,which are sciics,aio very slowly con\~ vergent；and m the case of orbits whose ecccntrcitics excccd 0 6627,or of orbits which have any radius of ono cqual to any radius of tho othcr the scrics aie diveigent and cannol bo uscd. The method of mechanical quadiatuies is cqually apphcablo lo all kinds of orbils,the only iestiiction being that the mlcrvals shall be taken sufficiently shoit It is the method actually cmployed,in one of its many foims,in compuling the poituibationg of the orbits of comets

The digadvantages aie that,in odei to find by mechamcal quadiatuics thc valucs of the elements at any paiticular time, it is neccssaiy to compute them at all of the inteimcdiate epochs. Bcing puicly nuinciical, il thiows no lght whatever on the general chaiaclei of peilubalions,and leads to no geneial theorems iegatding the stability of a syslemThese aie questions of gicnt inteicst,and somc of lhe most billant discoveries in Celestal Mcchanies have been made iespecting them

231.General Reflections.Astionomy is the oldest science and m n coi lain sonse thc paient of all the othersThe relatively simple and iegulauly iccuiing cclcstial phenomena fnst taught men,im thc days of thc ancient Giceks, that Nature is systematic and ordeilyThc importance of tlis lesson can be inferred from tho fact that it is the foundation on which all science is based For a long time progiess was painfully slowCenturies of observalions and ntiempts at thcorics fo1 explaining them were necessaiy befoic il was finally possible for Kepler to derive the Iaws which aio a fiist appioximalion to the dcscription of the way in which tlo planets move. The wonder is that,in spite of the distractions of tho constant sliuggles incident to an unstable Bocial ordel, thcre should have bcen so many men who found their greatest plensuro In pationtly making the laboiious observations which weio ncccssaiy to cstablsh the laws of the celestial motions

Tho woik of Keplcr closed the piclimmaiy epoch of two thousand ycars,or moro,and the billiant discoveries of Newton opened anothcr. Tho invention of the Calculus by Newton and Leibnitz fu nishcd for the firal time mathematical machinery which was al all sulable for grappling wilh such difficult problems as the disturbing cffccls of the sun on tho motion of the moon, or the mutual pcrturbalions of the planets. It was fortunate that the lelescopo was inventcd about the same time,for,without its use, it would nol havo bcen possible lo have made the accurate observations which furnishcd tho numerical data for the mathematical lhcorics and by which they were tested The history of Celestial Mechanics during thc cighicenth century is one of a continuous series of triumphs Tho analytical foundations laid by Clairaut, d'Alemboll,and Euler foimed the basis for the splendid achievemonls of Lagrange 'and Laplace. Their successors in the ninelconth centuiy pushcd forward,by the same methods on the whole,the lhcoiics of the motions of the moon and planets to highor oiders of approximation and compared them with more and better observations. In this connection the names of Leverrier,Delaunay,Hansen, and Newcomb will be espocially icmembered Near the close of the nineteenth contury a thnd epoch was entered. It is distinguished by new poinis of vicw and new methods whioh,in power and mathematical rigor,cnoimously surpass all those used beforeIt was inauguatcd by IIill m his Researches on the Lunar Theory,but owes most to the bnlliant contributions of Poincare to the Problem of Thico Bodics 1.1

At the present time Celestial Mechanics is entitlcd to be lcgaiaca is the most pelfeot scicnce and one of the most splendid achiovenents of the human mind. No othcr science is bascd on so many observations extending over so long a time. In no othci scicnco s it possible to test so critically its conclusions,and im no othcr are theory and expeiience in so perfect accord Tlore aic thousandg of small deviations fiom conic section motion in the orbits of the planets,satellites,and comcts where theoiy and the obscrvationg exactly agico,while the only unexplamcd iricgularitics (probably due to unknown foices) are a veiy fcw small oncs in the motion of the moon and the motion of the pciihelion of the orbit of Mercury. Over and over again theory has outrun practiso and indicated tho existcnce of pecularitics of motion which had not yet been dorived from obscrvations. Its perfection duing the time covered by experiencc inspics confidencc in following it back into the past to a time befoie observations began, and into the future to a time when perhaps they shall have ccasedAt the telescope has brought wilhin the range of thc cye of man thc wonders of an cnormous space, so Celestial Mcchamcs has bioughl within reach of his reason the no lesser wondcrs of a coriespond. ingly enormous time.
